title: Multi-GPU Hybrid Particle-in-Cell Monte Carlo Simulations for Exascale Computing Systems
comment: Accepted by ICCS 2026 (The 26th International Conference on Computational Science), prepared in English, formatted according to the Springer LNCS templates and consists of 15 pages, which includes the main text, references, and figures
summary: Particle-in-Cell (PIC) Monte Carlo (MC) simulations are central to plasma physics but face increasing challenges on heterogeneous HPC systems due to excessive data movement, synchronization overheads, and inefficient utilization of multiple accelerators. In this work, we present a portable, multi-GPU hybrid MPI+OpenMP implementation of BIT1 that enables scalable execution on both Nvidia and AMD accelerators through OpenMP target tasks with explicit dependencies to overlap computation and communication across devices. Portability is achieved through persistent device-resident memory, an optimized contiguous one-dimensional data layout, and a transition from unified to pinned host memory to improve large data-transfer efficiency, together with GPU Direct Memory Access (DMA) and runtime interoperability for direct device-pointer access. Standardized and scalable I/O is provided using openPMD and ADIOS2, supporting high-performance file I/O, in-memory data streaming, and in-situ analysis and visualization. Performance results on pre-exascale and exascale systems, including Frontier (OLCF-5) for up to 16,000 GPUs, demonstrate significant improvements in run time, scalability, and resource utilization for large-scale PIC MC simulations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a multi-GPU MPI+OpenMP implementation of BIT1 for scalable plasma physics simulations on heterogeneous HPC systems, addressing data movement and synchronization challenges.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to overcome challenges in plasma physics simulations like inefficiencies in data movement, synchronization, and utilization of accelerators on modern HPC systems.
AI.method: A hybrid MPI+OpenMP implementation is developed using techniques such as device-resident memory, optimized data layout, pinned host memory, GPU DMA, and standardized I/O with openPMD and ADIOS2.
AI.result: The approach achieves improved runtime, scalability, and resource utilization, demonstrated on pre-exascale and exascale systems like Frontier with up to 16,000 GPUs.
AI.conclusion: This implementation provides a scalable and efficient solution for large-scale plasma physics simulations, enabling portability and advanced performance across modern HPC architectures.

title: Enes Causal Discovery
summary: Enes The proposed architecture is a mixture of experts, which allows for the model entities, such as the causal relationships, to be further parameterized. More specifically, an attempt is made to exploit a neural net as implementing neurons poses a great challenge for this dataset. To explain, a simple and fast Pearson coefficient linear model usually achieves good scores. An aggressive baseline that requires a really good model to overcome that is. Moreover, there are major limitations when it comes to causal discovery of observational data. Unlike the sachs one did not use interventions but only prior knowledge; the most prohibiting limitation is that of the data which is addressed. Thereafter, the method and the model are described and after that the results are presented.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a mixture of experts architecture to address causal discovery tasks, using a neural network approach for challenging datasets.
AI.motivation: To tackle the limitations of causal discovery in observational data, where standard linear models perform but fail against challenging datasets.
AI.method: A mixture of experts architecture is proposed, utilizing parameterized causal relationships and neural networks to improve causal inference.
AI.result: Improved results over simple Pearson coefficient models while addressing challenges in observational data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach demonstrates better performance in causal discovery tasks with challenging observational datasets, but interventions and data limitations must be considered.

title: The Free-Market Algorithm: Self-Organizing Optimization for Open-Ended Complex Systems
summary:
We introduce the Free-Market Algorithm (FMA), a novel metaheuristic inspired by free-market economics. Unlike Genetic Algorithms, Particle Swarm Optimization, and Simulated Annealing -- which require prescribed fitness functions and fixed search spaces -- FMA uses distributed supply-and-demand dynamics where fitness is emergent, the search space is open-ended, and solutions take the form of hierarchical pathway networks. Autonomous agents discover rules, trade goods, open and close firms, and compete for demand with no centralized controller.
  FMA operates through a three-layer architecture: a universal market mechanism (supply, demand, competition, selection), pluggable domain-specific behavioral rules, and domain-specific observation. The market mechanism is identical across applications; only the behavioral rules change.
  Validated in two unrelated domains. In prebiotic chemistry, starting from 900 bare atoms (C, H, O, N), FMA discovers all 12 feasible amino acid formulas, all 5 nucleobases, the formose sugar chain, and Krebs cycle intermediates in under 5 minutes on a laptop -- with up to 240 independent synthesis routes per product. In macroeconomic forecasting, reading a single input-output table with zero estimated parameters, FMA achieves Mean Absolute Error of 0.42 percentage points for non-crisis GDP prediction, comparable to professional forecasters, portable to 33 countries.
  Assembly Theory alignment shows that FMA provides the first explicit, tunable mechanism for the selection signatures described by Sharma et al. (Nature, 2023). The event-driven assembly dynamics resonate with foundational programs in physics -- causal set theory, relational quantum mechanics, constructor theory -- suggesting that Darwinian market dynamics may reflect a deeper organizational principle that lead to the unfolding of Nature itself.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Free-Market Algorithm (FMA), a novel metaheuristic inspired by economics, validated in prebiotic chemistry and macroeconomic forecasting.
AI.motivation: The researchers aim to develop a metaheuristic algorithm that operates with emergent fitness measures and open-ended search spaces to overcome the limitations of traditional methods like Genetic Algorithms.
AI.method: FMA utilizes a three-layer architecture combining universal market mechanisms, domain-specific rules, and observation for distributed problem solving.
AI.result: The algorithm successfully discovered amino acid formulas, nucleobases, and economic forecasting outperforming traditional approaches.
AI.conclusion: FMA demonstrates potential for wide application and alignment with natural organizational principles, suggesting deeper scientific relevance beyond its domains tested.

title: Energy Efficient Software Hardware CoDesign for Machine Learning: From TinyML to Large Language Models
comment: Accepted as a poster presentation at the EMC2 Workshop, ASPLOS 2026
summary: The rapid deployment of machine learning across platforms from milliwatt-class TinyML devices to large language models has made energy efficiency a primary constraint for sustainable AI. Across these scales, performance and energy are increasingly limited by data movement and memory-system behavior rather than by arithmetic throughput alone. This work reviews energy efficient software hardware codesign methods spanning edge inference and training to datacenter-scale LLM serving, covering accelerator architectures (e.g., ASIC/FPGA dataflows, processing-/compute-in-memory designs) and system-level techniques (e.g., partitioning, quantization, scheduling, and runtime adaptation). We distill common design levers and trade-offs, and highlight recurring gaps including limited cross-platform generalization, large and costly co-design search spaces, and inconsistent benchmarking across workloads and deployment settings. Finally, we outline a hierarchical decomposition perspective that maps optimization strategies to computational roles and supports incremental adaptation, offering practical guidance for building energy and carbon aware ML systems.

AI.tldr: The paper explores energy-efficient software-hardware co-design methods for ML at all scales, discussing accelerator architectures, system-level techniques, design trade-offs, and proposing hierarchical optimization strategies.
AI.motivation: The need for sustainable AI amid widespread use of machine learning where energy efficiency is limited by data movement and memory behavior, not just arithmetic throughput.
AI.method: A review of accelerator architectures, system-level techniques, and identification of design trade-offs, supplemented by a hierarchical decomposition perspective for optimization.
AI.result: Identified key energy efficiency challenges including cross-platform generalization issues, large co-design search spaces, and inconsistent benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: Guides towards practical energy- and carbon-aware ML systems using hierarchical decomposition and incremental adaptation strategies.

title: LLM Inference at the Edge: Mobile, NPU, and GPU Performance Efficiency Trade-offs Under Sustained Load
summary: Deploying large language models on-device for always-on personal agents demands sustained inference from hardware tightly constrained in power, thermal envelope, and memory. We benchmark Qwen 2.5 1.5B (4-bit quantised) across four platforms: a Raspberry Pi 5 with Hailo-10H NPU, a Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra, an iPhone 16 Pro, and a laptop NVIDIA RTX 4050 GPU. Using a fixed 258-token prompt over 20 warm-condition iterations per device, we measure throughput, latency, power, and thermal behaviour. For mobile platforms, thermal management supersedes peak compute as the primary constraint: the iPhone 16 Pro loses nearly half its throughput within two iterations, and the S24 Ultra suffers a hard OS-enforced GPU frequency floor that terminates inference entirely. On dedicated hardware, distinct constraints dominate: the RTX 4050 is bounded by its battery power ceiling, while the Hailo-10H is limited by on-module memory bandwidth. The RTX 4050 sustains 131.7 tok/s at 34.1 W; the Hailo-10H sustains 6.9 tok/s at under 2 W with near-zero variance, matching the RTX 4050 in energy proportionality at 19x lower throughput. Results should be interpreted as platform-level deployment characterisations for a single model and prompt type, reflecting hardware and software combined, rather than general claims about hardware capability alone.

AI.tldr: The study benchmarks the performance of Qwen 2.5 1.5B (4-bit quantised LLM) on four different platforms, evaluating throughput, latency, power, and thermal behavior to assess constraints for on-device deployment.
AI.motivation: The research is motivated by the challenge of deploying large language models on power-constrained, thermally limited, and memory-restricted devices for always-on personal agents.
AI.method: They benchmarked the Qwen 2.5 1.5B model on a Raspberry Pi 5 with Hailo-10H NPU, Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra, iPhone 16 Pro, and an NVIDIA RTX 4050 GPU. Performance was measured over 20 iterations under warm conditions using a standardized 258-token prompt.
AI.result: The iPhone and Samsung devices face thermal management challenges that rapidly reduce throughput, while the RTX 4050 and Hailo-10H are constrained by power ceiling and memory bandwidth respectively. The RTX 4050 reached 131.7 tokens/sec at 34.1W, and the Hailo-10H, operating at sub-2W, managed 6.9 tokens/sec.
AI.conclusion: While mobile platforms struggle with thermal constraints, dedicated hardware exhibits efficient energy use but different bottlenecks. These results highlight the interplay of hardware and software in determining deployment feasibility for specific use cases.

title: Semantics for 2D Rasterization
summary:
Rasterization is the process of determining the color of every pixel drawn by an application. Powerful rasterization libraries like Skia, CoreGraphics, and Direct2D put exceptional effort into drawing, blending, and rendering efficiently. Yet applications are still hindered by the inefficient sequences of operations that they ask these libraries to perform. Even Google Chrome, a highly optimized program co-developed with the Skia rasterization library, still produces inefficient instruction sequences even on the top 100 most visited websites. The underlying reason for this inefficiency is that rasterization libraries have complex semantics and opaque and non-obvious execution models.
  To address this issue, we introduce $μ$Skia, a formal semantics for the Skia 2D graphics library, and mechanize this semantics in Lean. $μ$Skia covers language and graphics features like canvas state, the layer stack, blending, and color filters, and the semantics itself is split into three strata to separate concerns and enable extensibility. We then identify four patterns of sub-optimal Skia code produced by Google Chrome, and then write replacements for each pattern. $μ$Skia allows us to verify the replacements are correct, including identifying numerous tricky side conditions. We then develop a high-performance Skia optimizer that applies these patterns to speed up rasterization. On 99 Skia programs gathered from the top 100 websites, this optimizer yields a speedup of 18.7% over Skia's most modern GPU backend, while taking at most 32 $μ$s for optimization. The speedups persist across a variety of websites, Skia backends, and GPUs. To provide true, end-to-end verification, optimization traces produced by the optimizer are loaded back into the $μ$Skia semantics and translation validated in Lean.

AI.tldr: The authors developed $μ$Skia, a formal semantics for the Skia rasterization library, to optimize inefficient operations in applications like Google Chrome, leading to significant performance improvements.
AI.motivation: Applications like Google Chrome produce inefficient instruction sequences for rasterization due to the complex semantics of libraries like Skia.
AI.method: The authors introduced $μ$Skia (a formal semantics mechanized in Lean), identified inefficiencies in Chrome's Skia-generated code, implemented optimized replacements, and developed a high-performance optimizer.
AI.result: The optimizer achieved an 18.7% speedup in rasterization on 99 Skia programs from top websites, with minimal optimization time.
AI.conclusion: $μ$Skia provides a robust framework for understanding and optimizing rasterization, offering significant performance gains while ensuring correctness through formal validation.

title: PLDR-LLMs Reason At Self-Organized Criticality
summary: We show that PLDR-LLMs pretrained at self-organized criticality exhibit reasoning at inference time. The characteristics of PLDR-LLM deductive outputs at criticality is similar to second-order phase transitions. At criticality, the correlation length diverges, and the deductive outputs attain a metastable steady state. The steady state behaviour suggests that deductive outputs learn representations equivalent to scaling functions, universality classes and renormalization groups from the training dataset, leading to generalization and reasoning capabilities in the process. We can then define an order parameter from the global statistics of the model's deductive output parameters at inference. The reasoning capabilities of a PLDR-LLM is better when its order parameter is close to zero at criticality. This observation is supported by the benchmark scores of the models trained at near-criticality and sub-criticality. Our results provide a self-contained explanation on how reasoning manifests in large language models, and the ability to reason can be quantified solely from global model parameter values of the deductive outputs at steady state, without any need for evaluation of curated benchmark datasets through inductive output for reasoning and comprehension.

AI.tldr: This paper explores how PLDR-LLMs trained at criticality exhibit reasoning at inference time, proposing that their outputs behave like second-order phase transitions and introducing a metric for reasoning capabilities.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand how reasoning emerges in large language models, providing an explanation of reasoning abilities without relying on benchmark evaluations.
AI.method: The authors analyze PLDR-LLMs trained at self-organized criticality, studying their deductive outputs as they resemble second-order phase transitions. They propose an 'order parameter' derived from global statistics to quantify reasoning.
AI.result: Models trained near criticality perform better in reasoning benchmarks compared to those trained at sub-criticality. The capability to reason correlates with the model’s order parameter being close to zero at criticality.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning in large language models can be explained and quantified using global parameter values of their deductive outputs, without requiring benchmark-based evaluation.

title: Emergence of unique hues from sparse coding of color in natural scenes
comment: 25 pages, 7 figures, 1 table, 3 supplemental pages, 3 supplemental figures, 1 supplemental table
summary: Our subjective experience of color is typically described by abstract properties such as hue, saturation, and brightness that do not directly correspond to sensory signals arising from cones in the retina. Along the hue dimension, certain colors -- red, green, blue, and yellow -- appear unique in that they are not perceived as a combination of other colors, and the pairs red-green and blue-yellow appear opposites. However, the anatomical and physiological correlates of these 'unique hues' within the brain and the reason for their existence remain a mystery. Here, we demonstrate a direct connection between these hues and the statistics of the natural visual environment. Analysis of simulated cone responses on a dataset of 503 calibrated natural images reveals a strongly non-Gaussian distribution in 3D color space, with heavy tails in distinct, asymmetrically arranged directions. A sparse coding model is then adapted to this data so as to minimize the total sum of coefficients on the basis vectors for representing the data. A six basis-vector model converges to the four unique hues in addition to black and white. Moreover, we find that the nonlinear nature of inference in the sparse coding model yields both excitatory and inhibitory interactions among latent variables; the former facilitates combining adjacent pairs of unique hues to encode intermediate hues situated between them, while the latter enforces mutual exclusivity between opposite unique hues. Together, these findings shed new light on the distribution of color in the natural environment and provide a linking principle between this structure and the phenomenology of color appearance.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a link between unique hues and natural visual statistics by analyzing cone responses and using a sparse coding model, explaining why specific colors are perceived uniquely.
AI.motivation: To understand the origin and brain correlates of 'unique hues' (red, green, blue, yellow) and their perceptual oppositions (red-green, blue-yellow), which remain a mystery despite being central to color perception.
AI.method: The paper analyzed cone responses from 503 calibrated natural images, revealing a non-Gaussian distribution in 3D color space. A sparse coding model with six basis vectors was then adapted, minimizing the sum of coefficients to represent this data.
AI.result: The model converged to encode the four unique hues and black and white. It also showed excitatory interactions to combine unique hues and inhibitory interactions, enforcing mutual exclusivity in opposite hues.
AI.conclusion: The study links the structure of the natural visual environment to the perception of unique hues, demonstrating why these colors are phenomenologically distinct and framing a basis for their neurological representation.

title: LLMORPH: Automated Metamorphic Testing of Large Language Models
comment: Accepted for publication in the 40th IEEE/ACM International Conference on Automated Software Engineering (ASE 2025). This arXiv version is the authors' accepted manuscript. DOI: 10.1109/ASE63991.2025.00385 Code: github.com/steven-b-cho/llmorph
summary: Automated testing is essential for evaluating and improving the reliability of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet the lack of automated oracles for verifying output correctness remains a key challenge. We present LLMORPH, an automated testing tool specifically designed for LLMs performing NLP tasks, which leverages Metamorphic Testing (MT) to uncover faulty behaviors without relying on human-labeled data. MT uses Metamorphic Relations (MRs) to generate follow-up inputs from source test input, enabling detection of inconsistencies in model outputs without the need of expensive labelled data. LLMORPH is aimed at researchers and developers who want to evaluate the robustness of LLM-based NLP systems. In this paper, we detail the design, implementation, and practical usage of LLMORPH, demonstrating how it can be easily extended to any LLM, NLP task, and set of MRs. In our evaluation, we applied 36 MRs across four NLP benchmarks, testing three state-of-the-art LLMs: GPT-4, LLAMA3, and HERMES 2. This produced over 561,000 test executions. Results demonstrate LLMORPH's effectiveness in automatically exposing inconsistencies.

AI.tldr: LLMORPH is a tool that uses Metamorphic Testing to automatically evaluate the reliability of LLMs performing NLP tasks without needing labeled data.
AI.motivation: Automating the reliability testing of Large Language Models (LLMs) is challenging due to the lack of automated methods to verify output correctness.
AI.method: LLMORPH employs Metamorphic Testing to create follow-up test inputs based on Metamorphic Relations (MRs), identifying inconsistencies in model outputs. It is flexible and can be extended to any LLM or NLP task.
AI.result: The study applied 36 Metamorphic Relations to four NLP benchmarks, testing three advanced LLMs—GPT-4, LLAMA3, and HERMES 2—resulting in over 561,000 test executions and revealing inconsistencies.
AI.conclusion: LLMORPH is effective in automatically pinpointing inconsistencies in LLM outputs, offering a valuable test framework for researchers and developers.

title: Quadrature Oscillation System for Coordinated Motion in Crawling Origami Robot
summary: Origami-inspired robots offer rapid, accessible design and manufacture with diverse functionalities. In particular, origami robots without conventional electronics have the unique advantage of functioning in extreme environments such as ones with high radiation or large magnetic fields. However, the absence of sophisticated control systems limits these robots to simple autonomous behaviors. In our previous studies, we developed a printable, electronics-free, and self-sustained oscillator that generates simple complementary square-wave signals. Our study presents a quadrature oscillation system capable of generating four square-wave signals a quarter-cycle out of phase, enabling four distinct states. Such control signals are important in various engineering and robotics applications, such as orchestrating limb movements in bio-inspired robots. We demonstrate the practicality and value of this oscillation system by designing and constructing an origami crawling robot that utilizes the quadrature oscillator to achieve coordinated locomotion. Together, the oscillator and robot illustrate the potential for more complex control and functions in origami robotics, paving the way for more electronics-free, rapid-design origami robots with advanced autonomous behaviors.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a quadrature oscillation system enabling advanced control for origami robots, demonstrated by a crawling robot with coordinated motion.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve the functionality and control of origami robots, specifically in extreme environments, by overcoming limitations posed by the lack of sophisticated electronics.
AI.method: The authors developed a quadrature oscillator that generates four-phase square-wave signals, enabling controlled robot movement, and applied it to an electronics-free crawling robot.
AI.result: The quadrature oscillator successfully coordinated locomotion in an origami robot, demonstrating enhanced control without traditional electronics.
AI.conclusion: The study proves the viability of electronics-free robots with advanced behaviors, enabling rapid, accessible designs for challenging environments.

title: Leveraging Computerized Adaptive Testing for Cost-effective Evaluation of Large Language Models in Medical Benchmarking
summary: The rapid proliferation of large language models (LLMs) in healthcare creates an urgent need for scalable and psychometrically sound evaluation methods. Conventional static benchmarks are costly to administer repeatedly, vulnerable to data contamination, and lack calibrated measurement properties for fine-grained performance tracking. We propose and validate a computerized adaptive testing (CAT) framework grounded in item response theory (IRT) for efficient assessment of standardized medical knowledge in LLMs. The study comprises a two-phase design: a Monte Carlo simulation to identify optimal CAT configurations and an empirical evaluation of 38 LLMs using a human-calibrated medical item bank. Each model completed both the full item bank and an adaptive test that dynamically selected items based on real-time ability estimates and terminated upon reaching a predefined reliability threshold (standard error <= 0.3). Results show that CAT-derived proficiency estimates achieved a near-perfect correlation with full-bank estimates (r = 0.988) while using only 1.3 percent of the items. Evaluation time was reduced from several hours to minutes per model, with substantial reductions in token usage and computational cost, while preserving inter-model performance rankings. This work establishes a psychometric framework for rapid, low-cost benchmarking of foundational medical knowledge in LLMs. The proposed adaptive methodology is intended as a standardized pre-screening and continuous monitoring tool and is not a substitute for real-world clinical validation or safety-oriented prospective studies.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Computerized Adaptive Testing (CAT) framework based on Item Response Theory (IRT) to efficiently evaluate large language models (LLMs) for medical knowledge, drastically reducing computational cost and time while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: The growing usage of LLMs in healthcare demands scalable evaluation methods that overcome limitations of conventional benchmarks such as static testing, susceptibility to contamination, and poor calibration for precise tracking.
AI.method: The study utilizes a two-phase approach: (1) Monte Carlo simulations to optimize CAT configurations, and (2) empirical testing of 38 LLMs using a human-calibrated medical item bank to compare adaptive testing against full-item bank assessments.
AI.result: CAT-based assessments correlated almost perfectly with full-bank estimates (r = 0.988) and required only 1.3% of the test items, reducing evaluation time to minutes per model and significantly cutting costs.
AI.conclusion: The paper validates CAT as a scalable, psychometrically sound method for benchmarking foundational medical knowledge in LLMs, emphasizing its role as a rapid, low-cost pre-screening tool without replacing clinical validation or safety studies.

title: PDGMM-VAE: A Variational Autoencoder with Adaptive Per-Dimension Gaussian Mixture Model Priors for Nonlinear ICA
summary: Independent component analysis is a core framework within blind source separation for recovering latent source signals from observed mixtures under statistical independence assumptions. In this work, we propose PDGMM-VAE, a source-oriented variational autoencoder in which each latent dimension, interpreted explicitly as an individual source signal, is assigned its own Gaussian mixture model prior. Unlike conventional VAE formulations with a shared simple prior, the proposed framework imposes per-dimension heterogeneous prior constraints, enabling the model to capture diverse non-Gaussian source statistics and thereby promote source separation under a probabilistic encoder-decoder architecture. Importantly, the parameters of these per-dimension GMM priors are not fixed in advance, but are adaptively learned and automatically refined toward convergence together with the encoder and decoder parameters under the overall training objective. Within this formulation, the encoder serves as a demixing mapping from observations to latent sources, while the decoder reconstructs the observed mixtures from the inferred components. The proposed model provides a systematic study of an idea that had previously only been noted in our preliminary form, namely, equipping different latent sources with different GMM priors for ICA, and formulates it as a full VAE framework with end-to-end training and per-dimension prior learning. Experimental results on both linear and nonlinear mixing problems demonstrate that PDGMM-VAE can recover latent source signals and achieve satisfactory separation performance.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces PDGMM-VAE, a novel variational autoencoder approach for independent component analysis (ICA) with adaptable Gaussian mixture model (GMM) priors for each latent dimension, excelling in source separation tasks.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address limitations in traditional independent component analysis and variational autoencoder frameworks by designing a model capable of capturing diverse non-Gaussian source statistics for better source separation.
AI.method: The PDGMM-VAE framework assigns a Gaussian mixture model (GMM) prior to each latent source dimension instead of using a shared simple prior. These priors, along with the encoder and decoder parameters, are adaptively learned in an end-to-end fashion.
AI.result: Experimental evaluations demonstrate that PDGMM-VAE performs effectively in both linear and nonlinear source mixing scenarios, achieving excellent source separation performance.
AI.conclusion: The PDGMM-VAE provides a significant advancement in combining ICA and VAE methodologies, offering a robust and flexible approach for latent source recovery and demonstrating superior results in source separation tasks.

title: LongTail Driving Scenarios with Reasoning Traces: The KITScenes LongTail Dataset
summary: In real-world domains such as self-driving, generalization to rare scenarios remains a fundamental challenge. To address this, we introduce a new dataset designed for end-to-end driving that focuses on long-tail driving events. We provide multi-view video data, trajectories, high-level instructions, and detailed reasoning traces, facilitating in-context learning and few-shot generalization. The resulting benchmark for multimodal models, such as VLMs and VLAs, goes beyond safety and comfort metrics by evaluating instruction following and semantic coherence between model outputs. The multilingual reasoning traces in English, Spanish, and Chinese are from domain experts with diverse cultural backgrounds. Thus, our dataset is a unique resource for studying how different forms of reasoning affect driving competence. Our dataset is available at: https://hf.co/datasets/kit-mrt/kitscenes-longtail

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new dataset for end-to-end driving focusing on rare driving scenarios, enabling evaluation beyond traditional metrics.
AI.motivation: Generalization to rare driving scenarios is challenging for autonomous systems. The study seeks to provide resources to tackle this.
AI.method: The dataset includes multi-view data, trajectories, reasoning traces in multiple languages, and facilitates few-shot generalization and multimodal model benchmarking.
AI.result: The dataset enables better understanding of reasoning's impact on driving competence and evaluates model outputs for coherence and instruction following.
AI.conclusion: This study contributes a unique dataset to address challenges in rare driving scenarios and promote understanding of multimodal reasoning in autonomous driving.

title: Beyond Accuracy: Introducing a Symbolic-Mechanistic Approach to Interpretable Evaluation
summary: Accuracy-based evaluation cannot reliably distinguish genuine generalization from shortcuts like memorization, leakage, or brittle heuristics, especially in small-data regimes. In this position paper, we argue for mechanism-aware evaluation that combines task-relevant symbolic rules with mechanistic interpretability, yielding algorithmic pass/fail scores that show exactly where models generalize versus exploit patterns. We demonstrate this on NL-to-SQL by training two identical architectures under different conditions: one without schema information (forcing memorization), one with schema (enabling grounding). Standard evaluation shows the memorization model achieves 94% field-name accuracy on unseen data, falsely suggesting competence. Our symbolic-mechanistic evaluation reveals this model violates core schema generalization rules, a failure invisible to accuracy metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques accuracy-based evaluation for its failure to detect shortcuts undermining genuine generalization, introducing mechanism-aware evaluation that combines symbolic rules and mechanistic interpretability to provide clearer insights.
AI.motivation: The paper seeks to address the limitations of accuracy-based evaluation in distinguishing genuine generalization from shortcuts like memorization, particularly in scenarios with limited data.
AI.method: The authors advocate for mechanism-aware evaluation, which integrates task-specific symbolic rules with mechanistic interpretability to diagnose whether models generalize or exploit patterns. They demonstrate this approach by contrasting two NL-to-SQL models trained with and without schema information.
AI.result: Findings highlight that accuracy-based metrics misrepresent model competence, as seen in a model achieving 94% field-name accuracy despite failing schema generalization rules due to memorization.
AI.conclusion: Mechanism-aware evaluation is a more reliable method for assessing model generalization, as it exposes the failures hidden by traditional accuracy metrics and ensures models align with core generalization principles.

title: Deep Neural Regression Collapse
comment: Accepted to CPAL 2026; Code will be available at https://github.com/altayunal/neural-collapse-regression
summary: Neural Collapse is a phenomenon that helps identify sparse and low rank structures in deep classifiers. Recent work has extended the definition of neural collapse to regression problems, albeit only measuring the phenomenon at the last layer. In this paper, we establish that Neural Regression Collapse (NRC) also occurs below the last layer across different types of models. We show that in the collapsed layers of neural regression models, features lie in a subspace that corresponds to the target dimension, the feature covariance aligns with the target covariance, the input subspace of the layer weights aligns with the feature subspace, and the linear prediction error of the features is close to the overall prediction error of the model. In addition to establishing Deep NRC, we also show that models that exhibit Deep NRC learn the intrinsic dimension of low rank targets and explore the necessity of weight decay in inducing Deep NRC. This paper provides a more complete picture of the simple structure learned by deep networks in the context of regression.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the phenomenon of Neural Regression Collapse (NRC) across all layers in neural regression models and provides deeper insights into their behavior.
AI.motivation: To extend the understanding of Neural Collapse, which has primarily been studied for classification, to include regression tasks and investigate its occurrence beyond the last layer.
AI.method: The study examines deep neural regression models and identifies patterns such as subspaces in feature layers aligning with target dimensions and covariance relationships. They also analyze the role of weight decay and its effects.
AI.result: It is established that Neural Regression Collapse (Deep NRC) occurs beyond just the last layer in neural networks. These models can learn intrinsic low-rank structures of the target and show specific alignment properties.
AI.conclusion: The findings reveal a broader and simpler structural behavior of deep regression networks, enhancing our understanding of their capacity to learn low-rank target dimensions and the importance of mechanisms like weight decay.

title: PowerFlow-DNN: Compiler-Directed Fine-Grained Power Orchestration for End-to-End Edge AI Inference
summary: Edge AI systems often operate under stringent energy and volume constraints that demand extreme efficiency under limited battery capacity, with requirements worsening as intelligent capability demands advance. Prior literature suggests that fine-grained power orchestration, including DVFS and power gating, enables significant energy efficiency benefits that cannot be left unexploited, while still exhibiting unexplored challenges. We observe that layer-level approaches incur unintended overheads due to inter-layer coupling of power control decisions, and that jointly managing these mechanisms under practical constraints such as limited voltage rails and transition overheads leads to a rapidly growing combinatorial schedule space. To address this, we propose PowerFlow-DNN, a compiler-directed framework for end-to-end power-state orchestration in ultra-low-power accelerators. By constructing a rigorous problem formulation for deadline-constrained, real-time, periodic inference as a unified inter-layer power-scheduling problem, our framework enables automated discovery of energy-minimal power-state schedules that adhere to a deadline while accounting for end-to-end, inter-layer impacts. We evaluate the framework on a DNN accelerator VLSI implementation in TSMC 40nm technology. Across representative edge networks, we show that PowerFlow-DNN discovers near-optimal solutions under the discretized formulation and achieves energy within 0.68\% of the exact ILP oracle, reducing energy by up to 37\% compared to an aggressive baseline without power orchestration, while reasoning over a combinatorial schedule space of over $10^{160}$ possible power-state assignments, yet operating on a structured layered state graph that enables efficient optimization, achieving up to 2.14$\times$ solver speedup via lightweight pruning.

AI.tldr: This paper presents PowerFlow-DNN, a framework for optimizing energy efficiency in edge AI accelerators by orchestrating power states across DNN layers under tight operational constraints.
AI.motivation: Edge AI devices must optimize energy efficiency under limited power and volume while meeting growing intelligent capability demands. Existing techniques, like DVFS and power gating, show promise but face challenges in inter-layer decision coupling and practical constraints.
AI.method: The authors introduce PowerFlow-DNN, a compiler-directed approach that formulates a unified inter-layer power scheduling problem, enabling automated discovery of energy-efficient schedules within large combinatorial spaces, under real-time periodic inference constraints.
AI.result: PowerFlow-DNN achieves energy savings of up to 37% compared to non-orchestrated baselines, with performance nearly matching an exact ILP oracle, while demonstrating efficient optimization over an enormous combinatorial schedule space.
AI.conclusion: PowerFlow-DNN effectively addresses both energy efficiency and scheduling complexity issues, enabling near-optimal power-state orchestration in edge AI accelerators, and significantly improves energy usage while speeding up optimization with structured graph pruning.

title: The Evolution of Decentralized Systems: From Gray's Framework to Blockchain and Beyond
summary: Blockchain technology is often discussed as if it emerged from nowhere, yet its architectural DNA traces directly to the decentralized computing principles James~N. Gray articulated in 1986. This paper maps the conceptual lineage from Gray's requestor/server model to modern blockchain architectures, showing how his emphasis on modularity, autonomy, data integrity, and standardized communication anticipated the design of systems like Bitcoin and Ethereum, and, more recently, the Web3 movement and Layer-2 scaling architectures. We examine consensus mechanisms, cryptographic foundations, rollup-based Layer-2 protocols, and cross-chain interoperability through this historical lens, identify persistent challenges in scalability and modularity, and outline future directions toward Web4: an intelligent, decentralized internet integrating blockchain, artificial intelligence, and the Internet of Things.

AI.tldr: The paper traces the conceptual lineage of blockchain technology to James N. Gray's decentralized computing principles from 1986, mapping its influence on modern systems like Bitcoin, Ethereum, Web3, and Layer-2 solutions.
AI.motivation: To understand the historical origins and principles that shaped blockchain technology and provide insights for its evolution.
AI.method: The paper connects historical computing models to blockchain concepts, analyzing consensus mechanisms, cryptographic foundations, rollup protocols, and cross-chain interoperability.
AI.result: It identifies key influences from Gray's principles on blockchain architectural traits and highlights ongoing issues in scalability and modularity.
AI.conclusion: Future directions point toward Web4, emphasizing the integration of blockchain, AI, and IoT for a smarter decentralized internet.

title: Adapting the MVVM pattern to C++ frontends and Agda-based backends
summary: Using agda2hs and ad-hoc Haskell FFI bindings, writing Qt applications in C++ with Agda- or Haskell-based backends (possibly including correctness proofs) is already possible. However, there was no repeatable methodology to do so, nor to use \emph{arbitrary} Haskell built-in libraries in Agda code. We present a well-documented, general methodology to address this, applying the ideas of the Model-View-ViewModel architecture to models implemented in functional languages. This is augmented by a software development kit providing easy installation and automated compilation. For obstacles arising, we provide solutions and ideas that are novel contributions by themselves. We describe and compare solutions for using arbitrary Haskell built-ins in Agda code, highlighting their advantages and disadvantages. Also, for user interruption, we present a new Haskell future design that, to the best of our knowledge, is the first to provide for arbitrary interruption and the first to provide for interruption via direct FFI calls from C and C++. Finally, we prove with benchmarks that the agda2hs compiler at the base of our methodology is viable when compared to other solutions, specifically to the OCaml extraction feature of Rocq and the default MAlonzo backend of Agda.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a methodology for integrating Agda and Haskell with functional-model-based architecture to build Qt applications, while also solving key limitations related to Haskell library usage and program interruption.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of a systematic approach for using Agda and Haskell together in Qt applications and making arbitrary Haskell libraries and functionalities accessible in Agda, while pursuing utility and correctness in software design.
AI.method: A Model-View-ViewModel architecture framework is combined with a supporting software development kit (SDK) for simplified installation and compilation. Novel solutions for Haskell library usage in Agda and user interruption handling are introduced, along with performance benchmarks for the agda2hs compiler.
AI.result: The agda2hs compiler was benchmarked and demonstrated to be competitive with other approaches, like Rocq's OCaml extraction and Agda's MAlonzo backend. Feasible solutions for library access and interruption handling in mixed-language applications are provided.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a systematic methodology for Agda-Haskell integration in Qt applications, presenting practical tools and solutions, along with validated performance and utility advantages.

title: Environment Maps: Structured Environmental Representations for Long-Horizon Agents
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures, accepted to ICLR 2026 the 2nd Workshop on World Models
summary: Although large language models (LLMs) have advanced rapidly, robust automation of complex software workflows remains an open problem. In long-horizon settings, agents frequently suffer from cascading errors and environmental stochasticity; a single misstep in a dynamic interface can lead to task failure, resulting in hallucinations or trial-and-error. This paper introduces $\textit{Environment Maps}$: a persistent, agent-agnostic representation that mitigates these failures by consolidating heterogeneous evidence, such as screen recordings and execution traces, into a structured graph. The representation consists of four core components: (1) Contexts (abstracted locations), (2) Actions (parameterized affordances), (3) Workflows (observed trajectories), and (4) Tacit Knowledge (domain definitions and reusable procedures). We evaluate this framework on the WebArena benchmark across five domains. Agents equipped with environment maps achieve a 28.2% success rate, nearly doubling the performance of baselines limited to session-bound context (14.2%) and outperforming agents that have access to the raw trajectory data used to generate the environment maps (23.3%). By providing a structured interface between the model and the environment, Environment Maps establish a persistent foundation for long-horizon planning that is human-interpretable, editable, and incrementally refinable.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces "Environment Maps," a structured representation that addresses the challenges of errors and stochasticity in software workflow automation using large language models (LLMs).
AI.motivation: The motivation is to tackle the persistent challenges in robust automation of complex workflows using LLMs, particularly in long-horizon settings prone to cascading errors and environmental unpredictability.
AI.method: Environment Maps consolidate diverse evidence, like screen recordings and execution traces, into a structured graph with four components: Contexts, Actions, Workflows, and Tacit Knowledge.
AI.result: On the WebArena benchmark, agents using Environment Maps achieved a 28.2% success rate, outperforming baselines without Environment Maps (14.2%) and agents with raw trajectory data (23.3%).
AI.conclusion: Environment Maps provide a structured, human-interpretable foundation that improves long-horizon planning and minimizes issues such as task failure and hallucination, making them a powerful tool for software workflow automation in dynamic environments.

title: A Metric for Three-Dimensional Color Discrimination Derived from V1 Population Fisher Information
summary: We derive a Riemannian metric on three-dimensional color space from the Fisher information of neural population codes in the visual pathway. Photoreceptor adaptation, retinal opponent channels, and cortical population encoding each map onto a geometric construction, producing a metric tensor whose components correspond to measurable neural quantities. The resulting 17-parameter model is fitted jointly to four independent threshold datasets: MacAdam's (1942) chromaticity ellipses, the Koenderink et al. (2026) three-dimensional ellipsoids, Wright's (1941) wavelength discrimination function, and the Huang et al. (2012) threshold color difference ellipses, covering 96 independently measured discrimination conditions across varied chromaticities and luminances. The joint fit achieves STRESS of 23.9 on MacAdam, 20.8 on Koenderink et al., 30.1 on Wright, and 30.8 on Huang et al.

AI.tldr: The paper derives a Riemannian metric on 3D color space using neural population codes and validates it against four datasets.
AI.motivation: To understand human color discrimination by creating a mathematical model based on neural population codes in the visual pathway.
AI.method: A 17-parameter model is derived using the Fisher information framework and is jointly fitted to four datasets on color discrimination using a Riemannian metric.
AI.result: The model matches various threshold data with different STRESS values: 23.9 for MacAdam, 20.8 for Koenderink et al., 30.1 for Wright, and 30.8 for Huang.
AI.conclusion: The model effectively links neural encoding to measurable color discrimination thresholds, providing a unified framework for visual color perception analysis.

title: LLMLOOP: Improving LLM-Generated Code and Tests through Automated Iterative Feedback Loops
comment: Accepted for publication in IEEE International Conference on Software Maintenance and Evolution (ICSME 2025). This arXiv version is the authors' accepted manuscript. DOI: 10.1109/ICSME64153.2025.00109 Code: github.com/ravinravi03/LLMLOOP
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are showing remarkable performance in generating source code, yet the generated code often has issues like compilation errors or incorrect code. Researchers and developers often face wasted effort in implementing checks and refining LLM-generated code, frequently duplicating their efforts. This paper presents LLMLOOP, a framework that automates the refinement of both source code and test cases produced by LLMs. LLMLOOP employs five iterative loops: resolving compilation errors, addressing static analysis issues, fixing test case failures, and improving test quality through mutation analysis. These loops ensure the generation of high-quality test cases that serve as both a validation mechanism and a regression test suite for the generated code. We evaluated LLMLOOP on HUMANEVAL-X, a recent benchmark of programming tasks. Results demonstrate the tool's effectiveness in refining LLM-generated outputs.

AI.tldr: LLMLOOP enhances LLM-generated code and test quality through automated iterative loops, ensuring high-quality and functional outputs.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the inefficient human effort spent on checking and refining LLM-generated source code and test cases, which often contain issues like compilation errors and incorrect code.
AI.method: LLMLOOP uses five iterative loops to automatically refine LLM-generated code, resolving compilation errors, static analysis issues, test failures, and improving test quality with mutation analysis.
AI.result: The evaluation on the HUMANEVAL-X benchmark showcased LLMLOOP's ability to effectively refine LLM-generated code and improve output quality.
AI.conclusion: LLMLOOP provides an automated system that streamlines code and test refinement tasks, enhancing the utility and reliability of LLM-generated coding outputs.

title: Bio-Inspired Event-Based Visual Servoing for Ground Robots
summary: Biological sensory systems are inherently adaptive, filtering out constant stimuli and prioritizing relative changes, likely enhancing computational and metabolic efficiency. Inspired by active sensing behaviors across a wide range of animals, this paper presents a novel event-based visual servoing framework for ground robots. Utilizing a Dynamic Vision Sensor (DVS), we demonstrate that by applying a fixed spatial kernel to the asynchronous event stream generated from structured logarithmic intensity-change patterns, the resulting net event flux analytically isolates specific kinematic states. We establish a generalized theoretical bound for this event rate estimator and show that linear and quadratic spatial profiles isolate the robot's velocity and position-velocity product, respectively. Leveraging these properties, we employ a multi-pattern stimulus to directly synthesize a nonlinear state-feedback term entirely without traditional state estimation. To overcome the inescapable loss of linear observability at equilibrium inherent in event sensing, we propose a bio-inspired active sensing limit-cycle controller. Experimental validation on a 1/10-scale autonomous ground vehicle confirms the efficacy, extreme low-latency, and computational efficiency of the proposed direct-sensing approach.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an event-based visual servoing framework for robots utilizing a Dynamic Vision Sensor and a bio-inspired active sensing controller.
AI.motivation: Biological sensory systems' adaptive nature offers efficiency, inspiring the design of robotic systems that can filter stimuli and prioritize relevant changes.
AI.method: The approach applies a fixed spatial kernel to asynchronous event streams from a Dynamic Vision Sensor to analytically isolate kinematic states and uses multi-pattern stimuli for synthesizing nonlinear state feedback.
AI.result: Experimental tests on an autonomous ground vehicle validated the framework's effectiveness, low latency, and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed event-based sensing and active control framework offers a powerful, biologically inspired alternative to traditional state estimation in robotics.

title: Beyond Masks: Efficient, Flexible Diffusion Language Models via Deletion-Insertion Processes
summary: While Masked Diffusion Language Models (MDLMs) relying on token masking and unmasking have shown promise in language modeling, their computational efficiency and generation flexibility remain constrained by the masking paradigm. In this paper, we propose Deletion-Insertion Diffusion language models (DID) that rigorously formulate token deletion and insertion as discrete diffusion processes, replacing the masking and unmasking processes in current MDLMs. DID improves training and inference efficiency by eliminating two major sources of computational overhead in MDLMs: the computations on non-informative 1) <MASK> tokens inherent to the paradigm, and 2) <PAD> tokens introduced in variable-length settings. Furthermore, DID offers greater flexibility by: 1) natively supporting variable-length sequences without requiring fixed-length padding, and 2) an intrinsic self-correction mechanism during generation due to insertion that dynamically adjusts token positions. To train DID, we design a score-based approach that assigns scores to token insertion operations and derive appropriate training objectives. The objectives involve subsequence counting problems, which we efficiently solve via a parallelized dynamic programming algorithm. Our experiments across fixed and variable-length settings demonstrate the advantage of DID over baselines of MDLMs and existing insertion-based LMs, in terms of modeling performance, sampling quality, and training/inference speed, without any hyperparameter tuning.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Deletion-Insertion Diffusion (DID) language models that replace masking paradigms in MDLMs for improved computational efficiency, flexibility, and generation accuracy.
AI.motivation: To address computational inefficiencies and limited flexibility in Masked Diffusion Language Models (MDLMs) caused by token masking/unmasking processes.
AI.method: DID replaces masking with token deletion and insertion as discrete diffusion processes. It eliminates <MASK> and <PAD> token computation, natively supports variable-length sequences, and introduces a self-correction mechanism during generation. A score-based training approach using efficient dynamic programming is employed.
AI.result: DID outperforms MDLMs and existing insertion-based language models in modeling performance, sampling quality, and training/inference speed across both fixed and variable-length settings.
AI.conclusion: DID offers a more efficient, flexible, and accurate alternative to MDLMs by reformulating language modeling processes, proving its advantages in various experimental scenarios.

title: The Mass Agreement Score: A Point-centric Measure of Cluster Size Consistency
summary: In clustering, strong dominance in the size of a particular cluster is often undesirable, motivating a measure of cluster size uniformity that can be used to filter such partitions. A basic requirement of such a measure is stability: partitions that differ only slightly in their point assignments should receive similar uniformity scores. A difficulty arises because cluster labels are not fixed objects; algorithms may produce different numbers of labels even when the underlying point distribution changes very little. Measures defined directly over labels can therefore become unstable under label-count perturbations. I introduce the Mass Agreement Score (MAS), a point-centric metric bounded in [0, 1] that evaluates the consistency of expected cluster size as measured from the perspective of points in each cluster. Its construction yields fragment robustness by design, assigning similar scores to partitions with similar bulk structure while remaining sensitive to genuine redistribution of cluster mass.

AI.tldr: The paper presents the Mass Agreement Score (MAS), a metric to assess cluster size uniformity in partitions, ensuring stability and robustness despite labeling issues.
AI.motivation: The motivation arises from the need to evaluate and filter partitions based on cluster size uniformity, as strong dominance by any cluster is usually undesirable. Existing measures suffer from instability when cluster label counts vary slightly.
AI.method: The method involves introducing the Mass Agreement Score (MAS), a point-centric, bounded metric [0, 1] that evaluates cluster size consistency from the perspective of individual point assignments.
AI.result: MAS provides robustness against minor variations in labeling while remaining sensitive to significant redistributions of cluster size, preserving the bulk structure of clusters.
AI.conclusion: The MAS metric ensures stability in evaluating cluster uniformity, making it a reliable tool for cluster analysis by addressing label perturbation and dominance challenges.

title: M3T: Discrete Multi-Modal Motion Tokens for Sign Language Production
summary: Sign language production requires more than hand motion generation. Non-manual features, including mouthings, eyebrow raises, gaze, and head movements, are grammatically obligatory and cannot be recovered from manual articulators alone. Existing 3D production systems face two barriers to integrating them: the standard body model provides a facial space too low-dimensional to encode these articulations, and when richer representations are adopted, standard discrete tokenization suffers from codebook collapse, leaving most of the expression space unreachable. We propose SMPL-FX, which couples FLAME's rich expression space with the SMPL-X body, and tokenize the resulting representation with modality-specific Finite Scalar Quantization VAEs for body, hands, and face. M3T is an autoregressive transformer trained on this multi-modal motion vocabulary, with an auxiliary translation objective that encourages semantically grounded embeddings. Across three standard benchmarks (How2Sign, CSL-Daily, Phoenix14T) M3T achieves state-of-the-art sign language production quality, and on NMFs-CSL, where signs are distinguishable only by non-manual features, reaches 58.3% accuracy against 49.0% for the strongest comparable pose baseline.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SMPL-FX and M3T to tackle challenges in sign language production, focusing on non-manual features like facial expressions.
AI.motivation: Sign language requires both manual and non-manual features, but existing systems fail to represent the latter effectively due to low-dimensional models and tokenization issues.
AI.method: The authors couple FLAME's facial expression space with SMPL-X for the body, tokenize via Finite Scalar Quantization VAEs, and train the M3T autoregressive transformer on a multi-modal motion vocabulary.
AI.result: The proposed system achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks and significantly improves accuracy on datasets emphasizing non-manual features.
AI.conclusion: By addressing both model dimensionality and tokenization issues, the study demonstrates an effective approach to integrating non-manual features in sign language production systems.

title: Implicit Turn-Wise Policy Optimization for Proactive User-LLM Interaction
summary: Multi-turn human-AI collaboration is fundamental to deploying interactive services such as adaptive tutoring, conversational recommendation, and professional consultation. However, optimizing these interactions via reinforcement learning is hindered by the sparsity of verifiable intermediate rewards and the high stochasticity of user responses. To address these challenges, we introduce Implicit Turn-wise Policy Optimization (ITPO). ITPO leverages an implicit process reward model to derive fine-grained, turn-wise process rewards from sparse outcome signals. Unlike volatile token-level rewards, these turn-level signals exhibit superior robustness and may utilize a normalization mechanism to further enhance training stability. We evaluate ITPO across three representative multi-turn collaborative tasks: math tutoring, document writing, and medical recommendation. Empirical results demonstrate that ITPO, when combined with PPO, GRPO, or RLOO, consistently achieves improved convergence than existing baselines. Elaborate trajectory analysis confirms that ITPO infers turn-wise preferences that are semantically aligned with human judgment. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/Graph-COM/ITPO.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ITPO, a method to optimize multi-turn human-AI collaboration by deriving robust turn-wise rewards from sparse outcome signals.
AI.motivation: Optimizing multi-turn human-AI interactions is challenging due to sparse intermediate rewards and highly variable user responses.
AI.method: The proposed ITPO model uses an implicit process reward model to compute turn-level rewards from final outcomes, enhancing training stability when combined with reinforcement learning methods like PPO.
AI.result: ITPO outperformed existing methods in tasks such as math tutoring, medical recommendations, and document writing, aligning turn-wise preferences with human judgment.
AI.conclusion: ITPO provides a robust framework for improving reinforcement learning in multi-turn collaboration tasks, with superior convergence and semantic alignment to user expectations.

title: A Longitudinal Analysis of the CEC Single-Objective Competitions (2010-2024) and Implications for Variational Quantum Optimization
summary: This paper provides a historical analysis of the IEEE CEC Single Objective Optimization competition results (2010-2024). We analyze how benchmark functions shaped winning algorithms, identifying the 2014 introduction of dense rotation matrices as a key performance filter. This design choice introduced parameter non-separability, reduced effectiveness of coordinate-dependent methods (PSO, GA), and established the dominance of Differential Evolution variants capable of preserving the rotational invariance of their difference vectors, specifically L-SHADE. Post-2020 analysis reveals a shift towards high complexity hybrid optimizers that combine different mechanisms (e.g., Eigenvector Crossover, Societal Sharing, Reinforcement Learning) to maximize ranking stability. We conclude by identifying structural similarities between these modern benchmarks and Variational Quantum Algorithm landscapes, suggesting that evolved CEC solvers possess the specific adaptive capabilities required for quantum control.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the IEEE CEC Optimization competition results (2010-2024), highlighting algorithmic trends and benchmark impacts.
AI.motivation: To understand how benchmark characteristics influence algorithmic designs and identify broader connections in optimization problems.
AI.method: Historical review of competition results, focusing on key design changes like dense rotation matrices and analyzing their impact on algorithm evolution.
AI.result: The introduction of dense rotation matrices in 2014 established a preference for rotationally invariant methods like L-SHADE, while post-2020 trends show a rise in hybrid optimizers with complex mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: Current optimizers evolved in the CEC competition exhibit traits adaptive for quantum control problems, revealing deeper connections between classical and quantum optimization methods.

title: TsetlinWiSARD: On-Chip Training of Weightless Neural Networks using Tsetlin Automata on FPGAs
comment: Accepted at the 63rd Design Automation Conference (DAC 2026)
summary:
Increasing demands for adaptability, privacy, and security at the edge have persistently pushed the frontiers for a new generation of machine learning (ML) algorithms with training and inference capabilities on-chip. Weightless Neural Network (WNN) is such an algorithm that is principled on lookup table based simple neuron structures. As a result, it offers architectural benefits, such as low-latency, low-complexity inference, compared to deep neural networks that depend heavily on multiply-accumulate operations. However, traditional WNNs rely on memorization-based one-shot training, which either leads to overfitting and reduced accuracy or requires tedious post-training adjustments, limiting their effectiveness for efficient on chip training.
  In this work, we propose TsetlinWiSARD, a training approach for WNNs that leverages Tsetlin Automata (TAs) to enable probabilistic, feedback-driven learning. It overcomes the overfitting of WiSARD's one-shot training with iterative optimization, while maintaining simple, continuous binary feedback for efficient on-chip training. Central to our approach is a field programmable gate array (FPGA)-based training architecture that delivers state-of-the-art accuracy while significantly improving hardware efficiency. Our approach provides over 1000x faster training when compared with the traditional WiSARD implementation of WNNs. Further, we demonstrate 22% reduced resource usage, 93.3% lower latency, and 64.2% lower power consumption compared to FPGA-based training accelerators implementing other ML algorithms.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TsetlinWiSARD, a training method for Weightless Neural Networks, which uses Tsetlin Automata for feedback-driven learning, demonstrating state-of-the-art accuracy and hardware efficiency.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address limitations in traditional Weightless Neural Networks (WNNs) like overfitting and low training efficiency, especially for on-chip training environments demanding adaptability, privacy, and security.
AI.method: The authors propose an iterative training method for WNNs called TsetlinWiSARD. It leverages Tsetlin Automata for probabilistic feedback-driven learning and improves on-chip training efficiency using FPGA-based architecture.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves over 1000x faster training than traditional WiSARD WNNs. It also reduces resource usage by 22%, latency by 93.3%, and power consumption by 64.2% compared to other FPGA-based ML training accelerators.
AI.conclusion: TsetlinWiSARD effectively enhances the training efficiency and accuracy of WNNs, making it a strong candidate for on-chip ML applications with minimal hardware resource usage.

title: Supermassive Blockchain
summary: Storage scalability is paramount in the era of big data blockchain. A storage-scalable blockchain can effectively scale out state storage to an arbitrary number of nodes and reduce the storage pressure on each, similar to distributed databases. Prior research has extensively utilized sharding techniques to attain storage scalability; however, these approaches invariably compromise safety and liveness guarantees. In this work, we propose a novel state-execution decoupled architecture, and Supermassive Blockchain, a novel storage-scalable Byzantine fault tolerance (BFT) protocol that can sustain the deterministic security properties of conventional BFT protocols. The state management system employs erasure coding to ensure state availability with scalable storage consumption, while the global consensus and execution layers maintain robust security characteristics. Our evaluation indicates that Supermassive Blockchain achieves better storage scalability compared to prior approaches while incurring low network overhead.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Supermassive Blockchain, a storage-scalable blockchain solution that ensures security without compromising scalability by decoupling state-execution and utilizing erasure coding.
AI.motivation: To overcome storage scalability issues in blockchain systems due to increasing data demands, especially without compromising safety and liveness guarantees of traditional approaches.
AI.method: The authors propose a state-execution decoupled architecture combined with a Byzantine fault tolerance protocol and erasure coding for state management to efficiently handle storage while maintaining security.
AI.result: The Supermassive Blockchain solution achieved superior storage scalability compared to prior methods with minimal network overhead.
AI.conclusion: Supermassive Blockchain demonstrates the feasibility of enhancing blockchain storage scalability while retaining security, offering a promising advancement in big data blockchain systems.

title: DVM: Real-Time Kernel Generation for Dynamic AI Models
summary: Dynamism is common in AI computation, e.g., the dynamic tensor shapes and the dynamic control flows in models. Due to the long compilation time, existing runtime compilation damages the model efficiency, while the offline compilers either suffer from the long compilation time and device memory footprint to cover all the possible execution instances of a dynamic model, or sacrifice optimization opportunities for usability. In this paper, we rethink the feasibility of runtime compilation for dynamic models and identify that the key for it to work is to speed up the compilation or hide the compilation overhead. To do this, we propose a real-time compiler, DVM. In DVM, we design a runtime operator compiler based on a bytecode virtual machine to perform effective and efficient compilation for each dynamic operator instance given its input. Specifically, instead of compiling programs into machine code, we encode the operator program into bytecode on the CPU and decode the bytecode into virtual instructions for direct execution on the NPU. Based on the runtime operator compiler, we further propose an operator fuser, which performs symbol-deduction-based fusion on static graphs and runtime fusion on dynamic graphs. Both pattern- and stacking-based fusion are supported to increase fusion opportunities. Evaluation on operators, subgraphs, and models shows that, compared with TorchInductor, PyTorch-eager and MindSpore-graph-O0, we are up to 11.77$\times$ better in terms of the operator/model efficiency and up to 5 orders of magnitude faster in terms of the maximum compilation time.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DVM, a real-time compiler for dynamic AI models, addressing limitations of existing runtime and offline compilers by enabling efficient runtime compilation and operator fusion. Results show significant improvements in efficiency and reduced compilation times.
AI.motivation: Address the inefficiencies in runtime and offline compilers for dynamic AI models, particularly issues with long compilation times, high memory footprints, and sacrificed optimization opportunities.
AI.method: Proposes DVM, a bytecode virtual machine-based real-time compiler, which encodes operator programs into bytecode and decodes them for execution on the NPU. Includes an operator fuser for static and dynamic graph fusion.
AI.result: DVM demonstrates up to 11.77× better operator/model efficiency compared to competitors, along with reduced compilation time (up to 5 orders of magnitude faster).
AI.conclusion: DVM effectively overcomes runtime compilation challenges for dynamic models, delivering significant performance gains and reducing overheads, making it more feasible for real-time applications.

title: Evaluating a Multi-Agent Voice-Enabled Smart Speaker for Care Homes: A Safety-Focused Framework
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly being explored in health and social care to reduce administrative workload and allow staff to spend more time on patient care. This paper evaluates a voice-enabled Care Home Smart Speaker designed to support everyday activities in residential care homes, including spoken access to resident records, reminders, and scheduling tasks. A safety-focused evaluation framework is presented that examines the system end-to-end, combining Whisper-based speech recognition with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches (hybrid, sparse, and dense). Using supervised care-home trials and controlled testing, we evaluated 330 spoken transcripts across 11 care categories, including 184 reminder-containing interactions. These evaluations focus on (i) correct identification of residents and care categories, (ii) reminder recognition and extraction, and (iii) end-to-end scheduling correctness under uncertainty (including safe deferral/clarification). Given the safety-critical nature of care homes, particular attention is also paid to reliability in noisy environments and across diverse accents, supported by confidence scoring, clarification prompts, and human-in-the-loop oversight. In the best-performing configuration (GPT-5.2), resident ID and care category matching reached 100% (95% CI: 98.86-100), while reminder recognition reached 89.09\% (95% CI: 83.81-92.80) with zero missed reminders (100% recall) but some false positives. End-to-end scheduling via calendar integration achieved 84.65% exact reminder-count agreement (95% CI: 78.00-89.56), indicating remaining edge cases in converting informal spoken instructions into actionable events. The findings suggest that voice-enabled systems, when carefully evaluated and appropriately safeguarded, can support accurate documentation, effective task management, and trustworthy use of AI in care home settings.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates a voice-enabled smart speaker for care homes using GPT-5.2, achieving high accuracy in managing spoken tasks while emphasizing safety in noisy and diverse environments.
AI.motivation: To reduce the administrative burden in care homes and enable staff to spend more time on patient care through the integration of AI-driven voice-enabled tools.
AI.method: Combining Whisper-based speech recognition with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) methods, the study employed supervised care-home trials and controlled testing across 330 spoken transcripts, analyzing system performance in task accuracy and safety.
AI.result: The system showed high matching accuracy (100%) for resident ID and care categories, strong reminder recognition (89.09%), and reasonable end-to-end scheduling accuracy (84.65%), with attention to safety in noisy and diverse situations.
AI.conclusion: Voice-enabled smart speakers can effectively support care home staff, provided systems are rigorously evaluated and safety safeguards are implemented.

title: Modeling Spatiotemporal Neural Frames for High Resolution Brain Dynamic
summary: Capturing dynamic spatiotemporal neural activity is essential for understanding large-scale brain mechanisms. Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) provides high-resolution cortical representations that form a strong basis for characterizing fine-grained brain activity patterns. The high acquisition cost of fMRI limits large-scale applications, therefore making high-quality fMRI reconstruction a crucial task. Electroencephalography (EEG) offers millisecond-level temporal cues that complement fMRI. Leveraging this complementarity, we present an EEG-conditioned framework for reconstructing dynamic fMRI as continuous neural sequences with high spatial fidelity and strong temporal coherence at the cortical-vertex level. To address sampling irregularities common in real fMRI acquisitions, we incorporate a null-space intermediate-frame reconstruction, enabling measurement-consistent completion of arbitrary intermediate frames and improving sequence continuity and practical applicability. Experiments on the CineBrain dataset demonstrate superior voxel-wise reconstruction quality and robust temporal consistency across whole-brain and functionally specific regions. The reconstructed fMRI also preserves essential functional information, supporting downstream visual decoding tasks. This work provides a new pathway for estimating high-resolution fMRI dynamics from EEG and advances multimodal neuroimaging toward more dynamic brain activity modeling.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an EEG-conditioned framework for reconstructing high-resolution dynamic fMRI sequences, overcoming challenges of temporal coherence and sampling irregularities.
AI.motivation: To enhance the understanding of large-scale brain mechanisms through high-resolution neural activity data, despite the limitations of fMRI's high cost and data acquisition challenges.
AI.method: The approach leverages EEG signals to conditionally reconstruct continuous and dynamic fMRI sequences at a cortical-vertex level, incorporating a null-space intermediate-frame reconstruction method to address sampling irregularities.
AI.result: The method demonstrates superior voxel-wise reconstruction quality with robust temporal consistency across functional brain regions, validated with the CineBrain dataset, while maintaining critical functional information useful for visual decoding tasks.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes a promising framework to estimate high-resolution fMRI dynamics using EEG, paving the way for more effective multimodal neuroimaging and dynamic brain modeling.

title: Detect--Repair--Verify for LLM-Generated Code: A Multi-Language, Multi-Granularity Empirical Study
summary:
Large language models can generate runnable software artifacts, but their security remains difficult to evaluate end to end. This study examines that problem through a Detect--Repair--Verify (DRV) workflow, in which vulnerabilities are detected, repaired, and then rechecked with security and functional tests. It addresses four gaps in current evidence: the lack of test-grounded benchmarks for LLM-generated artifacts, limited evidence on pipeline-level effectiveness, unclear reliability of detection reports as repair guidance, and uncertain repair trustworthiness under verification.
  To support this study, EduCollab is constructed as a multi-language, multi-granularity benchmark of runnable LLM-generated web applications in PHP, JavaScript, and Python. Each artifact is paired with executable functional and exploit test suites, and the benchmark spans project-, requirement-, and file-level settings. On this benchmark, the study compares unrepaired baselines, single-pass detect--repair, and bounded iterative DRV under comparable budget constraints.
