AI.result: Evaluation across different environments showed superior task returns and better coordination, particularly in interaction bottleneck-heavy scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Automated reward design using language models can reduce manual engineering efforts while enhancing reward signal compatibility for cooperative learning systems.

title: A Sociolinguistic Analysis of Automatic Speech Recognition Bias in Newcastle English
summary:
Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems are widely used in everyday communication, education, healthcare, and industry, yet their performance remains uneven across speakers, particularly when dialectal variation diverges from the mainstream accents represented in training data. This study investigates ASR bias through a sociolinguistic analysis of Newcastle English, a regional variety of North-East England that has been shown to challenge current speech recognition technologies. Using spontaneous speech from the Diachronic Electronic Corpus of Tyneside English (DECTE), we evaluate the output of a state-of-the-art commercial ASR system and conduct a fine-grained analysis of more than 3,000 transcription errors. Errors are classified by linguistic domain and examined in relation to social variables including gender, age, and socioeconomic status. In addition, an acoustic case study of selected vowel features demonstrates how gradient phonetic variation contributes directly to misrecognition.
  The results show that phonological variation accounts for the majority of errors, with recurrent failures linked to dialect-specific features like vowel quality and glottalisation, as well as local vocabulary and non-standard grammatical forms. Error rates also vary across social groups, with higher error frequencies observed for men and for speakers at the extremes of the age spectrum. These findings indicate that ASR errors are not random but socially patterned and can be explained from a sociolinguistic perspective. Thus, the study demonstrates the importance of incorporating sociolinguistic expertise into the evaluation and development of speech technologies and argues that more equitable ASR systems require explicit attention to dialectal variation and community-based speech data.

AI.tldr: This paper examines ASR bias toward Newcastle English, revealing sociolinguistic factors affecting transcription errors.
AI.motivation: ASR systems struggle with transcription accuracy for dialectal variations like Newcastle English, highlighting a need for equitable solutions.
AI.method: The study evaluates a commercial ASR's output on Newcastle English using data from the DECTE corpus. Over 3,000 errors were analyzed across linguistic and social variables.
AI.result: Most errors are tied to phonological variation and sociodemographic factors like gender, age, and socioeconomic status.
AI.conclusion: ASR biases are socially patterned, underscoring the importance of addressing dialectal variation in speech technology development.

title: RefReward-SR: LR-Conditioned Reward Modeling for Preference-Aligned Super-Resolution
summary: Recent advances in generative super-resolution (SR) have greatly improved visual realism, yet existing evaluation and optimization frameworks remain misaligned with human perception. Full-Reference and No-Reference metrics often fail to reflect perceptual preference, either penalizing semantically plausible details due to pixel misalignment or favoring visually sharp but inconsistent artifacts. Moreover, most SR methods rely on ground-truth (GT)-dependent distribution matching, which does not necessarily correspond to human judgments. In this work, we propose RefReward-SR, a low-resolution (LR) reference-aware reward model for preference-aligned SR. Instead of relying on GT supervision or NR evaluation, RefReward-SR assesses high-resolution (HR) reconstructions conditioned on their LR inputs, treating the LR image as a semantic anchor. Leveraging the visual-linguistic priors of a Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLM), it evaluates semantic consistency and plausibility in a reasoning-aware manner. To support this paradigm, we construct RefSR-18K, the first large-scale LR-conditioned preference dataset for SR, providing pairwise rankings based on LR-HR consistency and HR naturalness. We fine-tune the MLLM with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) using LR-conditioned ranking rewards, and further integrate GRPO into SR model training with RefReward-SR as the core reward signal for preference-aligned generation. Extensive experiments show that our framework achieves substantially better alignment with human judgments, producing reconstructions that preserve semantic consistency while enhancing perceptual plausibility and visual naturalness. Code, models, and datasets will be released upon paper acceptance.

AI.tldr: The paper presents RefReward-SR, a new evaluation and optimization framework for super-resolution (SR) methods that is more aligned with human perception by using low-resolution images as semantic anchors and leveraging multimodal large language models (MLLM).
AI.motivation: Existing SR evaluation metrics and optimization strategies fail to align well with human perception, resulting in semantically inconsistent or less natural reconstructions.
AI.method: The proposed RefReward-SR uses low-resolution references to assess high-resolution outputs leveraging MLLM’s visual-linguistic priors and introduces a new dataset, RefSR-18K, for training and evaluation.
AI.result: RefReward-SR shows improved alignment with human judgments, producing reconstructions with better semantic consistency and visual naturalness.
AI.conclusion: RefReward-SR demonstrates significant potential to enhance SR methodologies through its human-perception-aligned approach, contributing to both evaluation and optimization improvements in the field.

title: Assessment Design in the AI Era: A Method for Identifying Items Functioning Differentially for Humans and Chatbots
summary: The rapid adoption of large language models (LLMs) in education raises profound challenges for assessment design. To adapt assessments to the presence of LLM-based tools, it is crucial to characterize the strengths and weaknesses of LLMs in a generalizable, valid and reliable manner. However, current LLM evaluations often rely on descriptive statistics derived from benchmarks, and little research applies theory-grounded measurement methods to characterize LLM capabilities relative to human learners in ways that directly support assessment design. Here, by combining educational data mining and psychometric theory, we introduce a statistically principled approach for identifying items on which humans and LLMs show systematic response differences, pinpointing where assessments may be most vulnerable to AI misuse, and which task dimensions make problems particularly easy or difficult for generative AI. The method is based on Differential Item Functioning (DIF) analysis -- traditionally used to detect bias across demographic groups -- together with negative control analysis and item-total correlation discrimination analysis. It is evaluated on responses from human learners and six leading chatbots (ChatGPT-4o \& 5.2, Gemini 1.5 \& 3 Pro, Claude 3.5 \& 4.5 Sonnet) to two instruments: a high school chemistry diagnostic test and a university entrance exam. Subject-matter experts then analyzed DIF-flagged items to characterize task dimensions associated with chatbot over- or under-performance. Results show that DIF-informed analytics provide a robust framework for understanding where LLM and human capabilities diverge, and highlight their value for improving the design of valid, reliable, and fair assessment in the AI era.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a robust statistical framework using Differential Item Functioning (DIF) analysis to assess disparities in performance between humans and large language models (LLMs) on educational tests, aiding in better assessment design.
AI.motivation: Due to the increasing role of LLMs in education and potential AI misuse, there is a need to evaluate their capabilities and weaknesses systematically to ensure fair and reliable assessments.
AI.method: The study applies DIF analysis, augmented with negative control and discrimination analysis, to compare response differences between humans and six leading chatbots on educational assessments.
AI.result: The method revealed systematic differences in performance between humans and LLMs, with subject-matter experts identifying task-specific dimensions responsible for LLMs' over- or under-performance.
AI.conclusion: DIF analysis offers a statistically grounded strategy for understanding and mitigating vulnerabilities in assessments affected by AI tools, ensuring fairness and reliability in education during the AI era.

title: MARCH: Multi-Agent Reinforced Self-Check for LLM Hallucination
summary: Hallucination remains a critical bottleneck for large language models (LLMs), undermining their reliability in real-world applications, especially in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. While existing hallucination detection methods employ LLM-as-a-judge to verify LLM outputs against retrieved evidence, they suffer from inherent confirmation bias, where the verifier inadvertently reproduces the errors of the original generation. To address this, we introduce Multi-Agent Reinforced Self-Check for Hallucination (MARCH), a framework that enforces rigorous factual alignment by leveraging deliberate information asymmetry. MARCH orchestrates a collaborative pipeline of three specialized agents: a Solver, a Proposer, and a Checker. The Solver generates an initial RAG response, which the Proposer decomposes into claim-level verifiable atomic propositions. Crucially, the Checker validates these propositions against retrieved evidence in isolation, deprived of the Solver's original output. This well-crafted information asymmetry scheme breaks the cycle of self-confirmation bias. By training this pipeline with multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), we enable the agents to co-evolve and optimize factual adherence. Extensive experiments across hallucination benchmarks demonstrate that MARCH substantially reduces hallucination rates. Notably, an 8B-parameter LLM equipped with MARCH achieves performance competitive with powerful closed-source models. MARCH paves a scalable path for factual self-improvement of LLMs through co-evolution. The code is at https://github.com/Qwen-Applications/MARCH.

AI.tldr: MARCH is a proposed framework to address hallucination in large language models by utilizing a multi-agent system to validate information, reducing the confirmation bias associated with existing methods.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to overcome hallucination in large language models, a major challenge that hampers their reliability in real-world applications, especially in RAG systems.
AI.method: MARCH utilizes a three-agent system (Solver, Proposer, Checker) in a collaborative framework, combined with multi-agent reinforcement learning to ensure accuracy and factual alignment.
AI.result: Experiments show MARCH significantly reduces hallucination rates, with an 8B-parameter model achieving results competitive with proprietary models.
AI.conclusion: MARCH represents a scalable solution for improving factual adherence in LLMs and reduces hallucinations through intentional information asymmetry and co-evolving agents.

title: Powerful Teachers Matter: Text-Guided Multi-view Knowledge Distillation with Visual Prior Enhancement
summary: Knowledge distillation transfers knowledge from large teacher models to smaller students for efficient inference. While existing methods primarily focus on distillation strategies, they often overlook the importance of enhancing teacher knowledge quality. In this paper, we propose Text-guided Multi-view Knowledge Distillation (TMKD), which leverages dual-modality teachers, a visual teacher and a text teacher (CLIP), to provide richer supervisory signals. Specifically, we enhance the visual teacher with multi-view inputs incorporating visual priors (edge and high-frequency features), while the text teacher generates semantic weights through prior-aware prompts to guide adaptive feature fusion. Additionally, we introduce vision-language contrastive regularization to strengthen semantic knowledge in the student model. Extensive experiments on five benchmarks demonstrate that TMKD consistently improves knowledge distillation performance by up to 4.49\%, validating the effectiveness of our dual-teacher multi-view enhancement strategy. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TMKD-main-44D1.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces TMKD, a method combining visual and text teachers to improve knowledge distillation by using multi-view enhancements and vision-language contrastive regularization.
AI.motivation: Current knowledge distillation methods often neglect improving the quality of teacher models’ knowledge, limiting their effectiveness for student models.
AI.method: The proposed TMKD framework enhances visual teachers with multi-view inputs (e.g., edge and high-frequency features) and uses text teachers like CLIP with semantic weighting via prior-aware prompts. It also incorporates vision-language contrastive regularization to improve semantic knowledge in the student model.
AI.result: TMKD achieves up to a 4.49% improvement in knowledge distillation performance across five benchmarks, proving its efficacy.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging dual-modality teachers and multi-view enhancements significantly boosts the effectiveness of knowledge distillation, offering a robust strategy for student model training.

title: MolEvolve: LLM-Guided Evolutionary Search for Interpretable Molecular Optimization
summary: Despite deep learning's success in chemistry, its impact is hindered by a lack of interpretability and an inability to resolve activity cliffs, where minor structural nuances trigger drastic property shifts. Current representation learning, bound by the similarity principle, often fails to capture these structural-activity discontinuities. To address this, we introduce MolEvolve, an evolutionary framework that reformulates molecular discovery as an autonomous, look-ahead planning problem. Unlike traditional methods that depend on human-engineered features or rigid prior knowledge, MolEvolve leverages a Large Language Model (LLM) to actively explore and evolve a library of executable chemical symbolic operations. By utilizing the LLM to cold start and an Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) engine for test-time planning with external tools (e.g. RDKit), the system self-discovers optimal trajectories autonomously. This process evolves transparent reasoning chains that translate complex structural transformations into actionable, human-readable chemical insights. Experimental results demonstrate that MolEvolve's autonomous search not only evolves transparent, human-readable chemical insights, but also outperforms baselines in both property prediction and molecule optimization tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MolEvolve, a framework utilizing Large Language Models and Monte Carlo Tree Search to autonomously discover and evolve actionable chemical insights, improving interpretability and performance in chemistry tasks.
AI.motivation: Deep learning in chemistry is hindered by poor interpretability and difficulty handling activity cliffs—situations where small molecular changes cause vast property shifts.
AI.method: MolEvolve combines an LLM for initializing chemical exploration with a Monte Carlo Tree Search for autonomous planning, evolving a library of chemical operations with external validation tools like RDKit.
AI.result: MolEvolve generates human-readable, transparent reasoning chains and outperforms conventional methods in property prediction and molecule optimization tasks.
AI.conclusion: MolEvolve successfully tackles issues of interpretability and activity cliffs by reformulating molecular discovery as an autonomous evolutionary planning problem, providing both actionable insights and superior task performance.

title: Retrieval Improvements Do Not Guarantee Better Answers: A Study of RAG for AI Policy QA
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems are increasingly used to analyze complex policy documents, but achieving sufficient reliability for expert usage remains challenging in domains characterized by dense legal language and evolving, overlapping regulatory frameworks. We study the application of RAG to AI governance and policy analysis using the AI Governance and Regulatory Archive (AGORA) corpus, a curated collection of 947 AI policy documents. Our system combines a ColBERT-based retriever fine-tuned with contrastive learning and a generator aligned to human preferences using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). We construct synthetic queries and collect pairwise preferences to adapt the system to the policy domain. Through experiments evaluating retrieval quality, answer relevance, and faithfulness, we find that domain-specific fine-tuning improves retrieval metrics but does not consistently improve end-to-end question answering performance. In some cases, stronger retrieval counterintuitively leads to more confident hallucinations when relevant documents are absent from the corpus. These results highlight a key concern for those building policy-focused RAG systems: improvements to individual components do not necessarily translate to more reliable answers. Our findings provide practical insights for designing grounded question-answering systems over dynamic regulatory corpora.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the use of retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems for analyzing AI policy documents, revealing challenges in ensuring reliability despite improvements in retrieval quality.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the challenges of using RAG systems in analyzing AI governance documents, which require handling dense legal language and overlapping regulatory frameworks.
AI.method: The method involves using a ColBERT-based retriever fine-tuned with contrastive learning, paired with a generator optimized via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), and evaluating the system with synthetic queries and pairwise preferences in the AI policy domain.
AI.result: Domain-specific fine-tuning enhanced retrieval metrics but did not consistently improve end-to-end QA performance. Stronger retrieval occasionally led to confident hallucinations when relevant documents were missing.
AI.conclusion: The findings emphasize that improving individual RAG components does not guarantee reliable answers, providing insights for designing robust QA systems for regulatory corpora.

title: HEART-PFL: Stable Personalized Federated Learning under Heterogeneity with Hierarchical Directional Alignment and Adversarial Knowledge Transfer
comment: Accepted at WACV 2026. 8 pages, 7 figures, 3 tables
summary: Personalized Federated Learning (PFL) aims to deliver effective client-specific models under heterogeneous distributions, yet existing methods suffer from shallow prototype alignment and brittle server-side distillation. We propose HEART-PFL, a dual-sided framework that (i) performs depth-aware Hierarchical Directional Alignment (HDA) using cosine similarity in the early stage and MSE matching in the deep stage to preserve client specificity, and (ii) stabilizes global updates through Adversarial Knowledge Transfer (AKT) with symmetric KL distillation on clean and adversarial proxy data. Using lightweight adapters with only 1.46M trainable parameters, HEART-PFL achieves state-of-the-art personalized accuracy on CIFAR-100, Flowers-102, and Caltech-101 (63.42%, 84.23%, and 95.67%, respectively) under Dirichlet non-IID partitions, and remains robust to out-of-domain proxy data. Ablation studies further confirm that HDA and AKT provide complementary gains in alignment, robustness, and optimization stability, offering insights into how the two components mutually reinforce effective personalization. Overall, these results demonstrate that HEART-PFL simultaneously enhances personalization and global stability, highlighting its potential as a strong and scalable solution for PFL(code available at https://github.com/danny0628/HEART-PFL).

AI.tldr: A novel framework called HEART-PFL for Personalized Federated Learning (PFL) improves client-specific models with enhanced accuracy, robustness, and stability using advanced alignment and distillation techniques.
AI.motivation: The paper tackles the challenge of Personalized Federated Learning (PFL), where achieving effective client-specific models under heterogeneous distributions is difficult due to limitations in existing methods like shallow prototype alignment and unstable server-side distillation.
AI.method: The proposed method, HEART-PFL, employs dual-sided strategies: (i) Hierarchical Directional Alignment (HDA) with cosine similarity and MSE matching for client specificity, and (ii) Adversarial Knowledge Transfer (AKT) with symmetric KL distillation using clean and adversarial proxy data.
AI.result: HEART-PFL demonstrates state-of-the-art performance with high personalized accuracy on datasets like CIFAR-100 (63.42%), Flowers-102 (84.23%), and Caltech-101 (95.67%) under Dirichlet non-IID partitions, while being robust to out-of-domain proxy data.
AI.conclusion: HEART-PFL effectively enhances personalization and global stability in PFL, offering a strong, scalable, and robust solution for improving client-specific models.

title: An In-Depth Study of Filter-Agnostic Vector Search on a PostgreSQL Database System: [Experiments and Analysis]
comment: 26 pages, 13 figures, to be published at SIGMOD 2026
summary:
Filtered Vector Search (FVS) is critical for supporting semantic search and GenAI applications in modern database systems. However, existing research most often evaluates algorithms in specialized libraries, making optimistic assumptions that do not align with enterprise-grade database systems. Our work challenges this premise by demonstrating that in a production-grade database system, commonly made assumptions do not hold, leading to performance characteristics and algorithmic trade-offs that are fundamentally different from those observed in isolated library settings. This paper presents the first in-depth analysis of filter-agnostic FVS algorithms within a production PostgreSQL-compatible system. We systematically evaluate post-filtering and inline-filtering strategies across a wide range of selectivities and correlations.
  Our central finding is that the optimal algorithm is not dictated by the cost of distance computations alone, but that system-level overheads that come from both distance computations and filter operations (like page accesses and data retrieval) play a significant role. We demonstrate that graph-based approaches (such as NaviX/ACORN) can incur prohibitive numbers of filter checks and system-level overheads, compared with clustering-based indexes such as ScaNN, often canceling out their theoretical benefits in real-world database environments.
  Ultimately, our findings provide the database community with crucial insights and practical guidelines, demonstrating that the optimal choice for a filter-agnostic FVS algorithm is not absolute, but rather a system-aware decision contingent on the interplay between workload characteristics and the underlying costs of data access in a real-world database architecture.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes Filtered Vector Search algorithms in production-grade PostgreSQL systems, showing that system-level costs like page accesses play a pivotal role in determining optimal choices rather than distance computation costs alone.
AI.motivation: Explore the performance of filter-agnostic Filtered Vector Search algorithms within enterprise-grade database systems, challenging the optimistic assumptions in isolated environments.
AI.method: In-depth systematic evaluation comparing post-filtering and inline-filtering strategies across selectivities and correlations in a production database system.
AI.result: Graph-based algorithms suffer from prohibitive system-level overheads, while clustering-based indexes often outperform these due to better alignment with real-world database costs.
AI.conclusion: The choice of optimal FVS algorithm is system-aware, depending on workload characteristics and real-world architectural overheads rather than theoretical computational efficiency alone.

title: RVLM: Recursive Vision-Language Models with Adaptive Depth
summary: Medical AI systems face two fundamental limitations. First, conventional vision-language models (VLMs) perform single-pass inference, yielding black-box predictions that cannot be audited or explained in clinical terms. Second, iterative reasoning systems that expose intermediate steps rely on fixed iteration budgets wasting compute on simple cases while providing insufficient depth for complex ones. We address both limitations with a unified framework. RVLM replaces single-pass inference with an iterative generate-execute loop: at each step, the model writes Python code, invokes vision sub-agents, manipulates images, and accumulates evidence. Every diagnostic claim is grounded in executable code, satisfying auditability requirements of clinical AI governance frameworks. RRouter makes iteration depth adaptive: a lightweight controller predicts the optimal budget from task-complexity features, then monitors progress and terminates early when reasoning stalls. We evaluate on BraTS 2023 Meningioma (brain MRI) and MIMIC-CXR (chest X-ray) using Gemini 2.5 Flash without fine-tuning. Across repeated runs, RVLM shows high consistency on salient findings (e.g., mass presence and enhancement) and can detect cross-modal discrepancies between Fluid-Attenuated Inversion Recovery (FLAIR) signal characteristics and segmentation boundaries. On MIMIC-CXR, it generates structured reports and correctly recognises view-specific artefacts. Code: https://github.com/nican2018/rvlm.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a vision-language model (RVLM) for medical imaging that uses an iterative, explainable reasoning process and an adaptive mechanism (RRouter) to optimize computational resources.
AI.motivation: To address limitations in current medical AI systems, namely the lack of explainability in conventional vision-language models and the inefficiency of fixed iteration reasoning systems.
AI.method: RVLM employs an iterative generate-execute loop, using Python code and vision sub-agents for reasoning. RRouter adaptively predicts iteration depth and halts reasoning when progress stagnates.
AI.result: RVLM demonstrates consistent diagnostic performance on brain MRI (BraTS 2023 Meningioma) and chest X-ray (MIMIC-CXR) datasets. It generates explainable diagnostic claims, detects cross-modal discrepancies, and identifies imaging artifacts.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully enhances both explanation and computational efficiency in medical imaging AI, addressing key limitations in current systems.

title: Marchuk: Efficient Global Weather Forecasting from Mid-Range to Sub-Seasonal Scales via Flow Matching
summary: Accurate subseasonal weather forecasting remains a major challenge due to the inherently chaotic nature of the atmosphere, which limits the predictive skill of conventional models beyond the mid-range horizon (approximately 15 days). In this work, we present \textit{Marchuk}, a generative latent flow-matching model for global weather forecasting spanning mid-range to subseasonal timescales, with prediction horizons of up to 30 days. Marchuk conditions on current-day weather maps and autoregressively predicts subsequent days' weather maps within the learned latent space. We replace rotary positional encodings (RoPE) with trainable positional embeddings and extend the temporal context window, which together enhance the model's ability to represent and propagate long-range temporal dependencies during latent forecasting. Marchuk offers two key advantages: high computational efficiency and strong predictive performance. Despite its compact architecture of only 276 million parameters, the model achieves performance comparable to LaDCast, a substantially larger model with 1.6 billion parameters, while operating at significantly higher inference speeds. We open-source our inference code and model at: https://v-gen-ai.github.io/Marchuk/

AI.tldr: Marchuk, a lightweight generative latent model, offers efficient and accurate mid-range to subseasonal global weather forecasting up to 30 days in advance.
AI.motivation: Accurate weather prediction for timescales beyond 15 days is challenging and essential for various applications, yet traditional models struggle with the chaotic nature of atmospheric dynamics.
AI.method: The model, named Marchuk, employs latent flow-matching and autoregressive predictions, augmented by trainable positional embeddings and extended temporal context windows for improved long-range temporal dependency handling.
AI.result: Marchuk achieves accuracy comparable to the significantly larger LaDCast model while maintaining higher computational efficiency and speed, with a compact architecture of only 276 million parameters.
AI.conclusion: Marchuk demonstrates the potential for efficient and accurate extended-range weather forecasting by leveraging novel generative modeling techniques, filling a gap left by conventional methods.

title: InstanceRSR: Real-World Super-Resolution via Instance-Aware Representation Alignment
comment: 4 pages, 4 figures, 2 tables. Accepted by ICASSP 2026
summary: Existing real-world super-resolution (RSR) methods based on generative priors have achieved remarkable progress in producing high-quality and globally consistent reconstructions. However, they often struggle to recover fine-grained details of diverse object instances in complex real-world scenes. This limitation primarily arises because commonly adopted denoising losses (e.g., MSE) inherently favor global consistency while neglecting instance-level perception and restoration. To address this issue, we propose InstanceRSR, a novel RSR framework that jointly models semantic information and introduces instance-level feature alignment. Specifically, we employ low-resolution (LR) images as global consistency guidance while jointly modeling image data and semantic segmentation maps to enforce semantic relevance during sampling. Moreover, we design an instance representation learning module to align the diffusion latent space with the instance latent space, enabling instance-aware feature alignment, and further incorporate a scale alignment mechanism to enhance fine-grained perception and detail recovery. Benefiting from these designs, our approach not only generates photorealistic details but also preserves semantic consistency at the instance level. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world benchmarks demonstrate that InstanceRSR significantly outperforms existing methods in both quantitative metrics and visual quality, achieving new state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance.

AI.tldr: InstanceRSR is a novel Real-world Super-Resolution (RSR) framework that improves fine-grained detail recovery for complex scenes using semantic modeling and instance-level feature alignment, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Existing RSR methods struggle to recover fine-grained details in complex real-world scenes due to denoising losses favoring global consistency over instance-level perception.
AI.method: InstanceRSR employs low-resolution images as guidance, models semantic segmentation maps, introduces instance representation learning for feature alignment, and incorporates a scale alignment mechanism for enhanced detail recovery.
AI.result: InstanceRSR demonstrates significant performance improvement in both quantitative metrics and visual quality, surpassing existing methods and achieving state-of-the-art results on real-world benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: InstanceRSR achieves photorealistic and semantically consistent reconstructions at the instance level, overcoming limitations of prior methods and setting a new standard for real-world super-resolution.

title: Learning Response-Statistic Shifts and Parametric Roll Episodes from Wave--Vessel Time Series via LSTM Functional Models
summary: Parametric roll is a rare but high-consequence instability that can trigger abrupt regime changes in ship response, including pronounced shifts in roll statistics and tail risk. This paper develops a data-driven surrogate that learns the nonlinear, causal functional mapping from incident wave--motion time series to vessel motions, and demonstrates that the surrogate reproduces both (i) parametric roll episodes and (ii) the associated statistical shifts in the response. Crucially, the learning framework is data-source agnostic: the paired wave--motion time series can be obtained from controlled experiments (e.g., towing-tank or basin tests with wave probes and motion tracking) when a hull exists, or from high-fidelity simulations during design when experiments are not yet available. To provide a controlled severe-sea demonstration, we generate training data with a URANS numerical wave tank, using long-crested irregular seas synthesized from a modified Pierson--Moskowitz spectrum. The demonstration dataset comprises 49 random-phase realizations for each of three sea states, simulated at a fixed forward speed selected to yield encounter conditions under which parametric-roll episodes can occur. A stacked LSTM surrogate is trained on wave-elevation time series and evaluated on held-out realizations using time-domain accuracy and distributional fidelity metrics. In the most severe case, the model tracks the onset and growth of large-amplitude roll consistent with parametric excitation, and captures the corresponding changes in roll probability density functions (PDFs). We further compare loss-function choices (MSE, relative-entropy-based objectives, and amplitude-weighted variants) and show how they trade average error for improved tail fidelity relevant to operability and risk assessment.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a data-driven surrogate model to predict ship motion responses, particularly focusing on parametric roll instabilities using wave-motion time series as input data.
AI.motivation: Parametric roll is a dangerous and rare instability in ship dynamics that can cause abrupt changes in risk and operability, necessitating better predictive and analytical tools.
AI.method: The paper develops a stacked LSTM surrogate model trained on wave-elevation time-series data generated from simulations using a URANS numerical wave tank, with varying loss functions for optimization.
AI.result: The model successfully replicates large-amplitude roll episodes and statistical distribution shifts, achieving high fidelity prediction, especially in severe wave conditions.
AI.conclusion: The surrogate model offers an effective framework for predicting ship responses to severe sea excitations, holding promise for risk assessment and operational planning.

title: B-MoE: A Body-Part-Aware Mixture-of-Experts "All Parts Matter" Approach to Micro-Action Recognition
summary: Micro-actions, fleeting and low-amplitude motions, such as glances, nods, or minor posture shifts, carry rich social meaning but remain difficult for current action recognition models to recognize due to their subtlety, short duration, and high inter-class ambiguity. In this paper, we introduce B-MoE, a Body-part-aware Mixture-of-Experts framework designed to explicitly model the structured nature of human motion. In B-MoE, each expert specializes in a distinct body region (head, body, upper limbs, lower limbs), and is based on the lightweight Macro-Micro Motion Encoder (M3E) that captures long-range contextual structure and fine-grained local motion. A cross-attention routing mechanism learns inter-region relationships and dynamically selects the most informative regions for each micro-action. B-MoE uses a dual-stream encoder that fuses these region-specific semantic cues with global motion features to jointly capture spatially localized cues and temporally subtle variations that characterize micro-actions. Experiments on three challenging benchmarks (MA-52, SocialGesture, and MPII-GroupInteraction) show consistent state-of-theart gains, with improvements in ambiguous, underrepresented, and low amplitude classes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces B-MoE, a specialized framework that recognizes subtle human micro-actions by using structured body-part motion analysis and dual-stream encoding.
AI.motivation: Recognizing micro-actions, such as nods and subtle gestures, is challenging for current action recognition models due to their subtlety, short duration, and ambiguity.
AI.method: B-MoE employs a Body-part-aware Mixture-of-Experts framework where each expert focuses on a specific body region. It uses a Macro-Micro Motion Encoder (M3E) for capturing contextual structure and fine-grained motion, along with a cross-attention routing mechanism to analyze inter-region relationships. A dual-stream encoder integrates local region-specific and global motion cues.
AI.result: B-MoE achieves state-of-the-art performance on three benchmarks (MA-52, SocialGesture, MPII-GroupInteraction) with noticeable improvements for ambiguous, underrepresented, and low-amplitude classes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed B-MoE framework effectively models and recognizes subtle micro-actions by leveraging body-region specialization and advanced motion encoding techniques.

title: CUA-Suite: Massive Human-annotated Video Demonstrations for Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-use agents (CUAs) hold great promise for automating complex desktop workflows, yet progress toward general-purpose agents is bottlenecked by the scarcity of continuous, high-quality human demonstration videos. Recent work emphasizes that continuous video, not sparse screenshots, is the critical missing ingredient for scaling these agents. However, the largest existing open dataset, ScaleCUA, contains only 2 million screenshots, equating to less than 20 hours of video. To address this bottleneck, we introduce CUA-Suite, a large-scale ecosystem of expert video demonstrations and dense annotations for professional desktop computer-use agents. At its core is VideoCUA, which provides approximately 10,000 human-demonstrated tasks across 87 diverse applications with continuous 30 fps screen recordings, kinematic cursor traces, and multi-layerfed reasoning annotations, totaling approximately 55 hours and 6 million frames of expert video. Unlike sparse datasets that capture only final click coordinates, these continuous video streams preserve the full temporal dynamics of human interaction, forming a superset of information that can be losslessly transformed into the formats required by existing agent frameworks. CUA-Suite further provides two complementary resources: UI-Vision, a rigorous benchmark for evaluating grounding and planning capabilities in CUAs, and GroundCUA, a large-scale grounding dataset with 56K annotated screenshots and over 3.6 million UI element annotations. Preliminary evaluation reveals that current foundation action models struggle substantially with professional desktop applications (~60% task failure rate). Beyond evaluation, CUA-Suite's rich multimodal corpus supports emerging research directions including generalist screen parsing, continuous spatial control, video-based reward modeling, and visual world models. All data and models are publicly released.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces CUA-Suite, a large-scale dataset with expert video demonstrations for building general-purpose computer-use agents. It includes 55 hours of video, comprehensive annotations, and benchmarks like UI-Vision to address current challenges in automating professional desktop workflows.
AI.motivation: The scarcity of continuous, high-quality human demonstration videos is a major bottleneck in advancing general-purpose computer-use agents. Existing datasets, like ScaleCUA, are limited in duration and content, making it difficult for agents to learn the full temporal dynamics of human interaction on desktop applications.
AI.method: The authors have developed CUA-Suite, which includes VideoCUA—a dataset with 10,000 human-demonstrated tasks across 87 applications. These continuous 30 fps recordings feature cursor traces and annotations. Additionally, CUA-Suite integrates two complementary resources: UI-Vision for benchmark testing and GroundCUA, a dataset with 56K screenshots and over 3.6 million UI element annotations.
AI.result: CUA-Suite provides a comprehensive ecosystem for training and evaluating CUAs. Preliminary assessments found that current action models face a ~60% task failure rate on professional desktop applications, highlighting the complexity of these environments and the potential of the proposed dataset.
AI.conclusion: CUA-Suite addresses critical gaps in existing datasets by offering continuous, annotated video and expansive benchmarks, enabling advanced research in areas like video-based reward modeling and generalist screen parsing. The dataset is publicly available to support further development of CUAs.

title: AI-driven Intent-Based Networking Approach for Self-configuration of Next Generation Networks
comment: Accepted for presentation in IEEE/IFIP NOMS 2026
summary: Intent-Based Networking (IBN) aims to simplify operating heterogeneous infrastructures by translating high-level intents into enforceable policies and assuring compliance. However, dependable automation remains difficult because (i) realizing intents from ambiguous natural language into controller-ready policies is brittle and prone to conflicts and unintended side effects, and (ii) assurance is often reactive and struggles in multi-intent settings where faults create cascading symptoms and ambiguous telemetry. This paper proposes an end-to-end closed-loop IBN pipeline that uses large language models with structured validation for natural language to policy realization and conflict-aware activation, and reformulates assurance as proactive multi-intent failure prediction with root-cause disambiguation. The expected outcome is operator-trustworthy automation that provides actionable early warnings, interpretable explanations, and measurable lead time for remediation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an improved Intent-Based Networking approach that uses AI for reliable policy translation and proactive fault prediction.
AI.motivation: The study addresses challenges in automating intent-based networking, such as ambiguity in natural language policies and difficulty in reactive assurance systems.
AI.method: The authors introduce a closed-loop IBN pipeline leveraging large language models, structured validation, and reformulated assurance to enhance policy realization and fault prediction.
AI.result: The proposed system aims to provide more reliable automation with features like early fault detection, clear explanations, and mitigation lead time.
AI.conclusion: An end-to-end AI-enhanced pipeline can improve operator trust in IBN systems by addressing ambiguities, conflicts, and fault prediction challenges.

title: Memory-Augmented Vision-Language Agents for Persistent and Semantically Consistent Object Captioning
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often yield inconsistent descriptions of the same object across viewpoints, hindering the ability of embodied agents to construct consistent semantic representations over time. Previous methods resolved inconsistencies using offline multi-view aggregation or multi-stage pipelines that decouple exploration, data association, and caption learning, with limited capacity to reason over previously observed objects. In this paper, we introduce a unified, memory-augmented Vision-Language agent that simultaneously handles data association, object captioning, and exploration policy within a single autoregressive framework. The model processes the current RGB observation, a top-down explored map, and an object-level episodic memory serialized into object-level tokens, ensuring persistent object identity and semantic consistency across extended sequences. To train the model in a self-supervised manner, we collect a dataset in photorealistic 3D environments using a disagreement-based policy and a pseudo-captioning model that enforces consistency across multi-view caption histories. Extensive evaluation on a manually annotated object-level test set, demonstrate improvements of up to +11.86% in standard captioning scores and +7.39% in caption self-similarity over baseline models, while enabling scalable performance through a compact scene representation. Code, model weights, and data are available at https://github.com/hsp-iit/epos-vlm

AI.tldr: The paper presents a memory-augmented Vision-Language agent with semantic consistency across views, improving object captioning and exploration within a unified framework.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language Models often fail to provide consistent descriptions for the same object across different viewpoints, creating challenges for embodied agents in constructing coherent semantic representations.
AI.method: The paper introduces a memory-augmented Vision-Language agent using an autoregressive framework that simultaneously handles data association, object captioning, and exploration. It incorporates RGB observations, top-down maps, and episodic memory into object-level representations, and trains with a self-supervised dataset leveraging a disagreement-based policy.
AI.result: The model achieves up to +11.86% improvement in captioning scores and +7.39% in caption self-similarity over baselines, supported by an annotated test set, while offering efficient scene representation.
AI.conclusion: The unified framework enhances semantic consistency and enables supportive scalability, promising improvements in embodied agent tasks requiring multi-view reasoning.

title: Conformalized Transfer Learning for Li-ion Battery State of Health Forecasting under Manufacturing and Usage Variability
comment: Submitted to the 2026 American Control Conference (ACC)
summary: Accurate forecasting of state-of-health (SOH) is essential for ensuring safe and reliable operation of lithium-ion cells. However, existing models calibrated on laboratory tests at specific conditions often fail to generalize to new cells that differ due to small manufacturing variations or operate under different conditions. To address this challenge, an uncertainty-aware transfer learning framework is proposed, combining a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model with domain adaptation via Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) and uncertainty quantification through Conformal Prediction (CP). The LSTM model is trained on a virtual battery dataset designed to capture real-world variability in electrode manufacturing and operating conditions. MMD aligns latent feature distributions between simulated and target domains to mitigate domain shift, while CP provides calibrated, distribution-free prediction intervals. This framework improves both the generalization and trustworthiness of SOH forecasts across heterogeneous cells.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an uncertainty-aware transfer learning framework combining LSTM, domain adaptation with MMD, and uncertainty quantification via CP to improve generalization and reliability of SOH forecasting for lithium-ion cells.
AI.motivation: Existing SOH models often fail to generalize across cells due to manufacturing variations and different operating conditions.
AI.method: The framework uses LSTM trained on a simulated battery dataset, MMD for aligning latent features, and CP for uncertainty intervals.
AI.result: The proposed approach enhances the generalization and trustworthiness of SOH predictions across diverse lithium-ion cells.
AI.conclusion: The uncertainty-aware framework addresses domain variability and provides reliable forecasting, advancing safe battery operation.

title: Accelerating Diffusion-based Video Editing via Heterogeneous Caching: Beyond Full Computing at Sampled Denoising Timestep
summary: Diffusion-based video editing has emerged as an important paradigm for high-quality and flexible content generation. However, despite their generality and strong modeling capacity, Diffusion Transformers (DiT) remain computationally expensive due to the iterative denoising process, posing challenges for practical deployment. Existing video diffusion acceleration methods primarily exploit denoising timestep-level feature reuse, which mitigates the redundancy in denoising process, but overlooks the architectural redundancy within the DiT that many attention operations over spatio-temporal tokens are redundantly executed, offering little to no incremental contribution to the model output. This work introduces HetCache, a training-free diffusion acceleration framework designed to exploit the inherent heterogeneity in diffusion-based masked video-to-video (MV2V) generation and editing. Instead of uniformly reuse or randomly sampling tokens, HetCache assesses the contextual relevance and interaction strength among various types of tokens in designated computing steps. Guided by spatial priors, it divides the spatial-temporal tokens in DiT model into context and generative tokens, and selectively caches the context tokens that exhibit the strongest correlation and most representative semantics with generative ones. This strategy reduces redundant attention operations while maintaining editing consistency and fidelity. Experiments show that HetCache achieves a noticeable acceleration, including a 2.67$\times$ latency speedup and FLOPs reduction over commonly used foundation models, with negligible degradation in editing quality.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HetCache, a training-free acceleration framework for Diffusion Transformers (DiT) in video editing, which significantly speeds up processing while maintaining editing quality.
AI.motivation: The motivation arises from the computationally expensive iterative denoising process of Diffusion Transformers (DiT) and redundant attention operations during spatio-temporal token handling, which hinder practical deployment.
AI.method: The proposed framework, HetCache, selectively caches context tokens that are most relevant and representative using spatial priors to assess contextual relevance and interaction strength between tokens, thus reducing redundant attention processes without extra training.
AI.result: HetCache provides a 2.67× latency speedup and reduces FLOPs with minimal degradation in video editing quality.
AI.conclusion: HetCache effectively accelerates diffusion-based video editing by reducing architectural redundancies in DiT, improving practical feasibility while maintaining fidelity and consistency.

title: Uniform Laws of Large Numbers in Product Spaces
summary:
Uniform laws of large numbers form a cornerstone of Vapnik--Chervonenkis theory, where they are characterized by the finiteness of the VC dimension. In this work, we study uniform convergence phenomena in cartesian product spaces, under assumptions on the underlying distribution that are compatible with the product structure. Specifically, we assume that the distribution is absolutely continuous with respect to the product of its marginals, a condition that captures many natural settings, including product distributions, sparse mixtures of product distributions, distributions with low mutual information, and more.
  We show that, under this assumption, a uniform law of large numbers holds for a family of events if and only if the linear VC dimension of the family is finite. The linear VC dimension is defined as the maximum size of a shattered set that lies on an axis-parallel line, namely, a set of vectors that agree on all but at most one coordinate. This dimension is always at most the classical VC dimension, yet it can be arbitrarily smaller. For instance, the family of convex sets in $\mathbb{R}^d$ has linear VC dimension $2$, while its VC dimension is infinite already for $d\ge 2$. Our proofs rely on estimator that departs substantially from the standard empirical mean estimator and exhibits more intricate structure. We show that such deviations from the standard empirical mean estimator are unavoidable in this setting. Throughout the paper, we propose several open questions, with a particular focus on quantitative sample complexity bounds.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces uniform laws of large numbers for cartesian product spaces under distributions compatible with the product structure. It finds that this uniform convergence is tied to a novel metric, the linear VC dimension, which differs substantially from classical VC dimension.
AI.motivation: To address uniform convergence phenomena in complex structured spaces like cartesian product spaces, particularly under natural distribution assumptions that often arise in real-world scenarios.
AI.method: A new metric, linear VC dimension, is introduced to evaluate uniform laws of large numbers. The proofs deviate from the standard empirical mean estimator, highlighting an alternative estimator structure necessary in this setting.
AI.result: The uniform laws hold in cartesian product spaces when the linear VC dimension is finite, with linear VC dimension often being significantly smaller than the classical VC dimension. For example, convex sets in $\mathbb{R}^d$ have finite linear VC dimension while their classical VC dimension is infinite.
AI.conclusion: Linear VC dimension provides a more nuanced and often tighter characterization of uniform convergence in structured spaces compared to classical VC dimension. The paper opens paths for future research, particularly around sample complexity bounds and estimator structures.

title: ScrollScape: Unlocking 32K Image Generation With Video Diffusion Priors
summary: While diffusion models excel at generating images with conventional dimensions, pushing them to synthesize ultra-high-resolution imagery at extreme aspect ratios (EAR) often triggers catastrophic structural failures, such as object repetition and spatial fragmentation.This limitation fundamentally stems from a lack of robust spatial priors, as static text-to-image models are primarily trained on image distributions with conventional dimensions.To overcome this bottleneck, we present ScrollScape, a novel framework that reformulates EAR image synthesis into a continuous video generation process through two core innovations.By mapping the spatial expansion of a massive canvas to the temporal evolution of video frames, ScrollScape leverages the inherent temporal consistency of video models as a powerful global constraint to ensure long-range structural integrity.Specifically, Scanning Positional Encoding (ScanPE) distributes global coordinates across frames to act as a flexible moving camera, while Scrolling Super-Resolution (ScrollSR) leverages video super-resolution priors to circumvent memory bottlenecks, efficiently scaling outputs to an unprecedented 32K resolution. Fine-tuned on a curated 3K multi-ratio image dataset, ScrollScape effectively aligns pre-trained video priors with the EAR generation task. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that it significantly outperforms existing image-diffusion baselines by eliminating severe localized artifacts. Consequently, our method overcomes inherent structural bottlenecks to ensure exceptional global coherence and visual fidelity across diverse domains at extreme scales.

AI.tldr: ScrollScape is a novel framework that synthesizes ultra-high-resolution images with extreme aspect ratios (EAR) by treating the process as video generation, leveraging video models' temporal consistency to overcome diffusion model limitations.
AI.motivation: The study is motivated by the limitations of existing diffusion models, which struggle with generating ultra-high-resolution images at extreme aspect ratios, often resulting in structural issues such as object repetition and spatial fragmentation.
AI.method: ScrollScape reformulates EAR image synthesis as continuous video generation. It introduces two innovations: Scanning Positional Encoding (ScanPE) for spatial-temporal mapping, and Scrolling Super-Resolution (ScrollSR) to scale outputs efficiently to 32K resolution.
AI.result: The proposed ScrollScape framework significantly outperforms baseline models in generating EAR images, eliminating localized artifacts and ensuring superior global coherence and visual fidelity.
AI.conclusion: ScrollScape successfully addresses the structural limitations of text-to-image models in EAR image generation, leveraging video generation techniques to achieve unprecedented resolution and quality.

title: Project and Generate: Divergence-Free Neural Operators for Incompressible Flows
summary: Learning-based models for fluid dynamics often operate in unconstrained function spaces, leading to physically inadmissible, unstable simulations. While penalty-based methods offer soft regularization, they provide no structural guarantees, resulting in spurious divergence and long-term collapse. In this work, we introduce a unified framework that enforces the incompressible continuity equation as a hard, intrinsic constraint for both deterministic and generative modeling. First, to project deterministic models onto the divergence-free subspace, we integrate a differentiable spectral Leray projection grounded in the Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition, which restricts the regression hypothesis space to physically admissible velocity fields. Second, to generate physically consistent distributions, we show that simply projecting model outputs is insufficient when the prior is incompatible. To address this, we construct a divergence-free Gaussian reference measure via a curl-based pushforward, ensuring the entire probability flow remains subspace-consistent by construction. Experiments on 2D Navier-Stokes equations demonstrate exact incompressibility up to discretization error and substantially improved stability and physical consistency.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a framework ensuring incompressible fluid dynamics simulations through hard constraints on divergence-free subspaces, enhancing physical consistency.
AI.motivation: Learning models often produce fluid dynamics simulations that are physically inadmissible due to unconstrained function spaces.
AI.method: The paper uses spectral Leray projection for deterministic models and constructs a divergence-free Gaussian distribution for generative models to enforce hard constraints on incompressibility.
AI.result: The experiments with 2D Navier-Stokes equations validate exact incompressibility within discretization error and improve overall stability.
AI.conclusion: The framework guarantees physically admissible fluid dynamics simulations, addressing stability and consistency issues observed with traditional methods.

title: The Cognitive Firewall:Securing Browser Based AI Agents Against Indirect Prompt Injection Via Hybrid Edge Cloud Defense
summary: Deploying large language models (LLMs) as autonomous browser agents exposes a significant attack surface in the form of Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI). Cloud-based defenses can provide strong semantic analysis, but they introduce latency and raise privacy concerns. We present the Cognitive Firewall, a three-stage split-compute architecture that distributes security checks across the client and the cloud. The system consists of a local visual Sentinel, a cloud-based Deep Planner, and a deterministic Guard that enforces execution-time policies. Across 1,000 adversarial samples, edge-only defenses fail to detect 86.9% of semantic attacks. In contrast, the full hybrid architecture reduces the overall attack success rate (ASR) to below 1% (0.88% under static evaluation and 0.67% under adaptive evaluation), while maintaining deterministic constraints on side-effecting actions. By filtering presentation-layer attacks locally, the system avoids unnecessary cloud inference and achieves an approximately 17,000x latency advantage over cloud-only baselines. These results indicate that deterministic enforcement at the execution boundary can complement probabilistic language models, and that split-compute provides a practical foundation for securing interactive LLM agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a Cognitive Firewall to safeguard Large Language Models (LLMs) from semantic attacks through a split-compute architecture, achieving significantly improved security and efficiency compared to cloud-only defenses.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the vulnerabilities of deploying LLMs as browser agents, particularly to Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) attacks, while overcoming latency and privacy issues presented by cloud-based defenses.
AI.method: The proposed solution employs a three-stage split-compute architecture comprising a local visual Sentinel, a cloud-based Deep Planner, and a deterministic Guard for policy enforcement.
AI.result: The full hybrid architecture reduced attack success rates to below 1% and improved latency by approximately 17,000x when compared to cloud-only methods.
AI.conclusion: Split-compute solutions, combining local and cloud layers, effectively secure LLM agents while balancing efficiency and privacy, demonstrating the viability of deterministic and probabilistic hybrid defenses.

title: TopoMesh: High-Fidelity Mesh Autoencoding via Topological Unification
summary: The dominant paradigm for high-fidelity 3D generation relies on a VAE-Diffusion pipeline, where the VAE's reconstruction capability sets a firm upper bound on generation quality. A fundamental challenge limiting existing VAEs is the representation mismatch between ground-truth meshes and network predictions: GT meshes have arbitrary, variable topology, while VAEs typically predict fixed-structure implicit fields (\eg, SDF on regular grids). This inherent misalignment prevents establishing explicit mesh-level correspondences, forcing prior work to rely on indirect supervision signals such as SDF or rendering losses. Consequently, fine geometric details, particularly sharp features, are poorly preserved during reconstruction. To address this, we introduce TopoMesh, a sparse voxel-based VAE that unifies both GT and predicted meshes under a shared Dual Marching Cubes (DMC) topological framework. Specifically, we convert arbitrary input meshes into DMC-compliant representations via a remeshing algorithm that preserves sharp edges using an L$\infty$ distance metric. Our decoder outputs meshes in the same DMC format, ensuring that both predicted and target meshes share identical topological structures. This establishes explicit correspondences at the vertex and face level, allowing us to derive explicit mesh-level supervision signals for topology, vertex positions, and face orientations with clear gradients. Our sparse VAE architecture employs this unified framework and is trained with Teacher Forcing and progressive resolution training for stable and efficient convergence. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TopoMesh significantly outperforms existing VAEs in reconstruction fidelity, achieving superior preservation of sharp features and geometric details.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TopoMesh, a technique addressing limitations in existing VAE-Diffusion pipelines for high-fidelity 3D generation by improving mesh reconstruction fidelity and sharp feature preservation.
AI.motivation: Existing VAEs struggle with mismatches between ground-truth (GT) meshes, which have arbitrary topology, and network predictions that rely on fixed structures. This mismatch hinders reconstruction and the preservation of fine geometric details, particularly sharp features.
AI.method: TopoMesh employs a shared topological framework between GT and predicted meshes using Dual Marching Cubes (DMC). Arbitrary input meshes are converted into DMC-compliant structures via a remeshing algorithm that preserves sharp edges. Using Teacher Forcing and progressive resolution training, the sparse VAE architecture enables mesh-level supervision with clear gradients at vertex and face levels.
AI.result: TopoMesh achieves significantly better reconstruction quality than existing VAEs, particularly in preserving sharp geometric features and intricate details.
AI.conclusion: The unified DMC-based approach in TopoMesh effectively removes representation mismatches between GT and predicted meshes, establishing explicit correspondences and markedly improving the reconstruction fidelity of 3D generation systems.

title: How Vulnerable Are Edge LLMs?
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed on edge devices under strict computation and quantization constraints, yet their security implications remain unclear. We study query-based knowledge extraction from quantized edge-deployed LLMs under realistic query budgets and show that, although quantization introduces noise, it does not remove the underlying semantic knowledge, allowing substantial behavioral recovery through carefully designed queries. To systematically analyze this risk, we propose \textbf{CLIQ} (\textbf{Cl}ustered \textbf{I}nstruction \textbf{Q}uerying), a structured query construction framework that improves semantic coverage while reducing redundancy. Experiments on quantized Qwen models (INT8/INT4) demonstrate that CLIQ consistently outperforms original queries across BERTScore, BLEU, and ROUGE, enabling more efficient extraction under limited budgets. These results indicate that quantization alone does not provide effective protection against query-based extraction, highlighting a previously underexplored security risk in edge-deployed LLMs.

AI.tldr: Quantization of large language models (LLMs) deployed on edge devices does not effectively protect against knowledge extraction via structured queries.
AI.motivation: To investigate the security risks associated with knowledge extraction from quantized edge-deployed LLMs.
AI.method: Proposed CLIQ (Clustered Instruction Querying), a query construction framework to enhance semantic coverage and reduce redundancy for efficient extraction.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate CLIQ's superior performance in semantic recovery from quantized models (INT8/INT4) across multiple metrics, like BERTScore, BLEU, and ROUGE.
AI.conclusion: Quantization introduces noise but does not eliminate semantic knowledge, emphasizing the need for additional measures to secure edge-deployed LLMs.

title: VERIA: Verification-Centric Multimodal Instance Augmentation for Long-Tailed 3D Object Detection
summary: Long-tail distributions in driving datasets pose a fundamental challenge for 3D perception, as rare classes exhibit substantial intra-class diversity yet available samples cover this variation space only sparsely. Existing instance augmentation methods based on copy-paste or asset libraries improve rare-class exposure but are often limited in fine-grained diversity and scene-context placement. We propose VERIA, an image-first multimodal augmentation framework that synthesizes synchronized RGB--LiDAR instances using off-the-shelf foundation models and curates them with sequential semantic and geometric verification. This verification-centric design tends to select instances that better match real LiDAR statistics while spanning a wider range of intra-class variation. Stage-wise yield decomposition provides a log-based diagnostic of pipeline reliability. On nuScenes and Lyft, VERIA improves rare-class 3D object detection in both LiDAR-only and multimodal settings. Our code is available at https://sgvr.kaist.ac.kr/VERIA/.

AI.tldr: VERIA is a framework that enhances rare-class 3D object detection by using multimodal augmentation with RGB and LiDAR data, leveraging foundation models, and applying rigorous semantic and geometric verification.
AI.motivation: Improving the detection of rare 3D object classes that suffer from sparse coverage and high intra-class variability in driving datasets.
AI.method: A multimodal augmentation framework generates synchronized RGB-LiDAR instances using foundation models and refines them with semantic and geometric verification.
AI.result: VERIA enhances rare-class detection performance in both LiDAR-only and multimodal settings on nuScenes and Lyft datasets.
AI.conclusion: The verification-centric augmentation process of VERIA achieves better alignment with realistic LiDAR statistics and expands the variation space, making it effective for rare-class 3D perception.

title: Claudini: Autoresearch Discovers State-of-the-Art Adversarial Attack Algorithms for LLMs
summary:
LLM agents like Claude Code can not only write code but also be used for autonomous AI research and engineering \citep{rank2026posttrainbench, novikov2025alphaevolve}. We show that an \emph{autoresearch}-style pipeline \citep{karpathy2026autoresearch} powered by Claude Code discovers novel white-box adversarial attack \textit{algorithms} that \textbf{significantly outperform all existing (30+) methods} in jailbreaking and prompt injection evaluations.
  Starting from existing attack implementations, such as GCG~\citep{zou2023universal}, the agent iterates to produce new algorithms achieving up to 40\% attack success rate on CBRN queries against GPT-OSS-Safeguard-20B, compared to $\leq$10\% for existing algorithms (\Cref{fig:teaser}, left).
  The discovered algorithms generalize: attacks optimized on surrogate models transfer directly to held-out models, achieving \textbf{100\% ASR against Meta-SecAlign-70B} \citep{chen2025secalign} versus 56\% for the best baseline (\Cref{fig:teaser}, middle). Extending the findings of~\cite{carlini2025autoadvexbench}, our results are an early demonstration that incremental safety and security research can be automated using LLM agents. White-box adversarial red-teaming is particularly well-suited for this: existing methods provide strong starting points, and the optimization objective yields dense, quantitative feedback. We release all discovered attacks alongside baseline implementations and evaluation code at https://github.com/romovpa/claudini.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates the effectiveness of using Claude Code, an LLM agent, in an autoresearch pipeline to autonomously discover novel white-box adversarial attacks that outperform existing methods in security evaluations.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to explore the capability of LLM agents like Claude Code in autonomous AI research, particularly in advancing the design and efficacy of adversarial attack algorithms.
AI.method: The pipeline leverages Claude Code in an autoresearch approach, starting from existing adversarial attack implementations. The agent iterates to discover new algorithms optimized on surrogate models and evaluates them for performance across various benchmarks.
AI.result: Discovered adversarial attacks significantly outperform existing methods, achieving success rates of 40% on GPT-OSS-Safeguard-20B and 100% on Meta-SecAlign-70B compared to much lower rates by baselines.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that LLMs can automate incremental safety and security research by leveraging existing strong methods and quantitative feedback. White-box adversarial red-teaming is particularly apt for such automation, and the paper provides public access to its discoveries and implementations.

title: RS-SSM: Refining Forgotten Specifics in State Space Model for Video Semantic Segmentation
summary: Recently, state space models have demonstrated efficient video segmentation through linear-complexity state space compression. However, Video Semantic Segmentation (VSS) requires pixel-level spatiotemporal modeling capabilities to maintain temporal consistency in segmentation of semantic objects. While state space models can preserve common semantic information during state space compression, the fixed-size state space inevitably forgets specific information, which limits the models' capability for pixel-level segmentation. To tackle the above issue, we proposed a Refining Specifics State Space Model approach (RS-SSM) for video semantic segmentation, which performs complementary refining of forgotten spatiotemporal specifics. Specifically, a Channel-wise Amplitude Perceptron (CwAP) is designed to extract and align the distribution characteristics of specific information in the state space. Besides, a Forgetting Gate Information Refiner (FGIR) is proposed to adaptively invert and refine the forgetting gate matrix in the state space model based on the specific information distribution. Consequently, our RS-SSM leverages the inverted forgetting gate to complementarily refine the specific information forgotten during state space compression, thereby enhancing the model's capability for spatiotemporal pixel-level segmentation. Extensive experiments on four VSS benchmarks demonstrate that our RS-SSM achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining high computational efficiency. The code is available at https://github.com/zhoujiahuan1991/CVPR2026-RS-SSM.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RS-SSM, a novel approach to improve video semantic segmentation (VSS) by refining forgotten specifics in state space models. It achieves superior spatiotemporal pixel-level segmentation and computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing state space models struggle with maintaining specific semantic details for pixel-level segmentation during video processing, limiting temporal consistency in object segmentation.
AI.method: The authors introduce RS-SSM, which uses two key components: Channel-wise Amplitude Perceptron (CwAP) for extracting and aligning specific information distributions; and Forgetting Gate Information Refiner (FGIR) for adaptively refining forgotten specifics in the state space.
AI.result: RS-SSM achieves state-of-the-art performance on four VSS benchmarks while retaining computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The RS-SSM approach significantly enhances pixel-level spatiotemporal segmentation in VSS, resolving the issue of forgotten specific information during state space compression.

title: AVO: Agentic Variation Operators for Autonomous Evolutionary Search
summary: Agentic Variation Operators (AVO) are a new family of evolutionary variation operators that replace the fixed mutation, crossover, and hand-designed heuristics of classical evolutionary search with autonomous coding agents. Rather than confining a language model to candidate generation within a prescribed pipeline, AVO instantiates variation as a self-directed agent loop that can consult the current lineage, a domain-specific knowledge base, and execution feedback to propose, repair, critique, and verify implementation edits. We evaluate AVO on attention, among the most aggressively optimized kernel targets in AI, on NVIDIA Blackwell (B200) GPUs. Over 7 days of continuous autonomous evolution on multi-head attention, AVO discovers kernels that outperform cuDNN by up to 3.5% and FlashAttention-4 by up to 10.5% across the evaluated configurations. The discovered optimizations transfer readily to grouped-query attention, requiring only 30 minutes of additional autonomous adaptation and yielding gains of up to 7.0% over cuDNN and 9.3% over FlashAttention-4. Together, these results show that agentic variation operators move beyond prior LLM-in-the-loop evolutionary pipelines by elevating the agent from candidate generator to variation operator, and can discover performance-critical micro-architectural optimizations that produce kernels surpassing state-of-the-art expert-engineered attention implementations on today's most advanced GPU hardware.

AI.tldr: Agentic Variation Operators (AVO) are a new approach to evolutionary optimization using autonomous agents rather than fixed mutation rules or preset heuristics. These agents improve kernel performance for AI models by consulting knowledge bases and execution feedback.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of traditional evolutionary search methods by creating a more autonomous optimization process that can self-direct and adapt to complex kernel-performance challenges.
AI.method: AVO introduces variation through autonomous agents capable of proposing, critiquing, and verifying implementation edits. These agents work continuously, using lineage data and domain knowledge for optimization.
AI.result: AVO achieved up to 10.5% performance gains over FlashAttention-4 and up to 3.5% over cuDNN for attention kernels on NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs. Optimizations transferred effectively to grouped-query attention, showing notable performance improvements with minimal adaptation time.
AI.conclusion: AVO elevates autonomous agents to active variation operators, enabling them to discover optimizations surpassing state-of-the-art expert-designed implementations without manual intervention.

title: ORACLE: Orchestrate NPC Daily Activities using Contrastive Learning with Transformer-CVAE
summary: The integration of Non-player characters (NPCs) within digital environments has been increasingly recognized for its potential to augment user immersion and cognitive engagement. The sophisticated orchestration of their daily activities, reflecting the nuances of human daily routines, contributes significantly to the realism of digital environments. Nevertheless, conventional approaches often produce monotonous repetition, falling short of capturing the intricacies of real human activity plans. In response to this, we introduce ORACLE, a novel generative model for the synthesis of realistic indoor daily activity plans, ensuring NPCs' authentic presence in digital habitats. Exploiting the CASAS smart home dataset's 24-hour indoor activity sequences, ORACLE addresses challenges in the dataset, including its imbalanced sequential data, the scarcity of training samples, and the absence of pre-trained models encapsulating human daily activity patterns. ORACLE's training leverages the sequential data processing prowess of Transformers, the generative controllability of Conditional Variational Autoencoders (CVAE), and the discriminative refinement of contrastive learning. Our experimental results validate the superiority of generating NPC activity plans and the efficacy of our design strategies over existing methods.

AI.tldr: The authors present ORACLE, a generative model to create realistic indoor activity plans for NPCs, improving immersion and engagement in digital environments using advanced machine learning techniques.
AI.motivation: Traditional methods for NPC activity planning fail to replicate the realism of human daily routines, leading to repetitive and unrealistic behaviors in digital environments.
AI.method: The approach employs the CASAS smart home dataset along with Transformers, CVAEs, and contrastive learning to generate detailed NPC activity plans, addressing challenges like imbalanced data and lack of pre-trained models.
AI.result: ORACLE outperforms existing methods in generating realistic NPC activity plans, demonstrating the effectiveness of its training strategies.
AI.conclusion: Using ORACLE improves the realism and immersion of NPC behavior in digital environments, addressing significant limitations of conventional approaches.

title: AMIF: Authorizable Medical Image Fusion Model with Built-in Authentication
summary: Multimodal image fusion enables precise lesion localization and characterization for accurate diagnosis, thereby strengthening clinical decision-making and driving its growing prominence in medical imaging research.
